topics.title: Overview of Computer Vision and Image Processing – Introduction to Computer Vision, Applications, History, and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Computer Vision and Image Processing – Introduction to Computer Vision, Applications, History, and Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Vision (CV):** Computer Vision is an interdisciplinary scientific field of Artificial Intelligence and Computer Science that deals with the theory, design, and implementation of algorithms by which meaningful information about an object, scene, or activity can be automatically extracted, analysed, and understood from one or more two-dimensional images of a real-world scene.

> [!NOTE]
> **Image Processing (IP):** Image Processing is a method or technique to perform some operations on an image, in order to get an enhanced image or to extract some useful information from it. It is a type of signal processing in which input is an image and output may be an image or features/characteristics associated with that image.

The two fields are tightly coupled but **fundamentally different** in objective:

| Aspect | Image Processing | Computer Vision |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | **Image-to-Image** transformation (enhancement, restoration) | **Image-to-Symbol/Decision** mapping (recognition, understanding) |
| Output | A processed image | Labels, bounding boxes, 3D models, semantic descriptions |
| Scope | Low-level & mid-level operations | High-level cognition and reasoning |
| Input Requirement | Needs an image as input | Can use images, video sequences, or multi-view geometry |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Eyewitness vs. Sketch Artist" comparison:**
>
> Think of a **human eye** as a camera. The **retina** captures light (Image Formation), the **visual cortex** in the brain processes the signal (Image Processing), and the **mind's interpretation** of "that is my friend standing under the tree" is the act of **Computer Vision**.
>
> - **Image Processing** is like a *sketch artist* who cleans up a noisy photograph, sharpens it, removes red-eye, and makes it visually pleasant.
> - **Computer Vision** is like a *detective* who looks at that cleaned-up photo and concludes: *"There are 3 people, one is carrying a black bag, the crime happened at night."*

### 1.3 Key Terminology (High-Yield for KTU)

- **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable unit of a digital image, with coordinates $(x, y)$ and an intensity value $f(x, y)$.
- **Resolution:** The number of pixels in an image, expressed as $M \times N$ (height × width).
- **Digitization:** The process of converting a continuous real-world scene into a discrete digital image, achieved through **Sampling** (spatial discretization) and **Quantization** (intensity discretization).
- **Grayscale Image:** A 2D function $f(x, y)$ where each pixel has a single intensity value.
- **Colour Image:** A composite of three 2D functions $f_R(x,y)$, $f_G(x,y)$, $f_B(x,y)$ or alternative colour spaces like HSV, Lab.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchical Levels of Computer Vision

Computer Vision problems are traditionally organized into three hierarchical levels. KTU examiners frequently ask classification questions based on this structure:

1. **Low-Level Vision (Image Processing Stage):**
   - Operates directly on raw pixel data.
   - Examples: Noise removal, edge detection, contrast enhancement, image sharpening.
   - Output is still an image.

2. **Mid-Level Vision (Feature Extraction Stage):**
   - Deals with segmentation, object detection, and feature description.
   - Examples: Thresholding, region growing, Hough transform, SIFT/SURF feature extraction.
   - Output is a set of attributes or geometric primitives.

3. **High-Level Vision (Cognition Stage):**
   - Involves recognition, interpretation, and semantic reasoning.
   - Examples: Face recognition, scene understanding, action recognition, autonomous driving decisions.
   - Output is a symbolic description or decision.

### 2.2 Image Formation — The Physics Behind the Pixels

For a real-world scene to become a digital image, two physical processes must occur:

- **Illumination Source ($i(x, y)$):** The amount of source light incident on the scene element being observed. Affected by lighting type (natural, ambient, directional).
- **Reflectance Component ($r(x, y)$):** The amount of light reflected by the object. Bounded physically: $0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$ (where 0 = total absorption/black, 1 = total reflection/white).

The combined image formation equation forms the foundation of all low-level vision:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

Where:
- $f(x, y)$ is the observed image intensity at spatial coordinate $(x, y)$.
- $i(x, y)$ is the illumination function.
- $r(x, y)$ is the reflectance function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$
> - $0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$
> - Therefore: $0 < f(x, y) < \infty$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Units / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image as 2D Function** | $f(x, y)$ with $x \in [0, M-1]$, $y \in [0, N-1]$ | Intensity (normalized 0 to 1) or 0–255 for 8-bit | Foundation of all digital image representation |
| **Image Formation Model** | $f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$ | Dimensionless, bounded $0 < f < \infty$ | Used in photometric stereo, intrinsic image decomposition |
| **Total Number of Pixels** | $N_{total} = M \times N$ | Pixels (px) | Camera sensor sizing, memory estimation |
| **Aspect Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | Dimensionless | Display compatibility, projection geometry |
| **Bit Depth (Storage)** | $B = M \times N \times k$ bits (for $k$-bit grayscale) | bits, bytes, KB, MB | File size estimation |
| **Number of Grey Levels** | $L = 2^k$ | Dimensionless | Quantization design |
| **Spatial Resolution (DPI)** | $DPI = \dfrac{\text{printable dots}}{\text{1 inch}}$ | dots per inch | Print quality, medical imaging standards |
| **Sampling Rate Relation** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist Criterion) | Pixels per mm or DPI | Anti-aliasing filter design, sensor selection |
| **SNR for Image Quality** | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) | Quality benchmarking of vision systems |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the KTU syllabus start here?**
> Every downstream CV task — from a face unlock on a phone to the lane-detection system in a KTU Autonomous Vehicle project — **must first understand the image formation pipeline**. Without the $f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$ model, concepts like **illumination invariance** (critical in Indian outdoor conditions), **HDR imaging** (for traffic monitoring in Kerala's monsoons), and **white balancing** would be mathematically unjustified.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Scene to Discrete Digital Image

The conversion of a real, continuous-world scene $f_c(x, y)$ into a digital image $f_d(x, y)$ is a two-step mathematical process. The following derivation is essential for KTU Module 1 derivations.

**Step 1 — Sampling (Spatial Discretization):**

We sample the continuous image function at uniform spatial intervals $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ along the x and y axes, respectively.

$$f_d(m, n) = f_c(m \cdot \Delta x, n \cdot \Delta y)$$

Where $m \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, M-1\}$ and $n \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\}$.

> **Interpretation:** Each pixel at integer coordinate $(m, n)$ represents the average intensity over a small spatial region $\Delta x \times \Delta y$ of the continuous scene.

**Step 2 — Quantization (Intensity Discretization):**

Each sampled continuous intensity value $f_c$ is mapped to one of $L$ discrete intensity levels.

$$f_q = \text{round}\left( \dfrac{f_c - f_{min}}{f_{max} - f_{min}} \cdot (L - 1) \right)$$

Where $L = 2^k$ and $k$ is the bit-depth (e.g., $k=8 \Rightarrow L=256$ grey levels for standard JPEG).

**Step 3 — Forming the Digital Image Matrix:**

The final digital image is an $M \times N$ matrix:

$$f_d = \begin{bmatrix} f(0,0) & f(0,1) & \cdots & f(0,N-1) \\ f(1,0) & f(1,1) & \cdots & f(1,N-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ f(M-1,0) & f(M-1,1) & \cdots & f(M-1,N-1) \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Storage Requirement of a Digital Image

A common KTU numerical problem is to calculate the memory required to store a digital image.

**Problem Setup:** Given a colour image of resolution $M \times N$ with bit-depth $k$ per channel and $C$ colour channels, derive the total storage size.

**Step 1:** Number of pixels in the image.

$$N_{total} = M \times N$$

**Step 2:** Total number of bits per pixel.

$$b_{pixel} = k \times C$$

**Step 3:** Total bits for the image.

$$B_{total} = M \times N \times k \times C$$

**Step 4:** Convert to bytes (divide by 8).

$$S_{bytes} = \dfrac{M \times N \times k \times C}{8}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to megabytes (MB), where $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \times 1024$ bytes.

$$S_{MB} = \dfrac{M \times N \times k \times C}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
> Calculate the storage size of a $1920 \times 1080$ RGB image with 8-bit depth per channel.

- $M = 1920$, $N = 1080$, $k = 8$, $C = 3$ (for RGB).
- $B_{total} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 8 \times 3 = 49,766,400$ bits.
- $S_{bytes} = 49,766,400 / 8 = 6,220,800$ bytes $\approx 5.93$ MB.

### 3.3 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Constraint Applied to Images

To avoid aliasing (e.g., the "wagon-wheel effect" in video), the sampling frequency in the spatial domain must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}^{spatial}$$

**Conversion to Pixel Pitch:**

If the highest spatial frequency in the scene corresponds to a feature of size $d_{min}$ in mm, then the sensor pixel pitch $p$ must satisfy:

$$p \leq \dfrac{d_{min}}{2}$$

This is why **medical X-ray imaging** uses pixel pitches of $50 \text{ }\mu m$ or smaller — the structures of interest (blood vessels) have features in the order of $100\text{–}200 \text{ }\mu m$.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (CV vs. IP vs. Pattern Recognition vs. AI)

This table is **frequently asked** in KTU Module 1 questions to test whether students understand the boundaries between closely related fields.

| Field | Input | Output | Core Question Answered | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Image Processing** | Image | Image | "How do I clean/enlarge this image?" | OpenCV, MATLAB |
| **Computer Vision** | Image(s) | Symbols/Labels/3D | "What is in this image and where?" | CNNs, YOLO, OpenCV |
| **Pattern Recognition** | Feature vector | Class label | "Which class does this feature belong to?" | SVM, k-NN, Random Forest |
| **Machine Learning / AI** | General data | Predictions | "What pattern/hypothesis explains this data?" | TensorFlow, PyTorch |
| **Image Analysis (Medical)** | Image | Quantitative measurement | "How large is the tumour in mm?" | ITK, SimpleITK |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Computer Vision Pipeline — End-to-End Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the canonical sequence of operations in any Computer Vision system — from scene capture to high-level decision-making.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Scene] --> B[Illumination Source]
    B --> C[Object Reflectance]
    C --> D[Image Sensor / Camera]
    D --> E[Sampling and Quantization]
    E --> F[Digital Image as Matrix]
    F --> G[Low Level Image Processing]
    G --> G1[Noise Removal]
    G --> G2[Contrast Enhancement]
    G --> G3[Edge Detection]
    G1 --> H[Mid Level Feature Extraction]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> H1[Segmentation]
    H --> H2[Keypoint Detection SIFT ORB]
    H --> H3[Feature Descriptors]
    H1 --> I[High Level Recognition]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> I1[Object Classification]
    I --> I2[Object Tracking]
    I --> I3[3D Scene Reconstruction]
    I1 --> J[Decision or Action]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Application Output]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Nodes `A`, `B`, `C` represent the **physical world** (image formation phase governed by $f(x,y)=i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$).
- Nodes `D`, `E`, `F` represent the **digitization phase** (sampling and quantization).
- Nodes `G`, `H`, `I` represent the **three hierarchical levels of CV** (low, mid, high).
- Node `J` represents the **decision-making layer** (e.g., "Apply brake", "Authenticate user", "Tumour detected").

### 4.2 Computer Vision vs. Human Vision — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HumanVision[Human Vision System]
        HV1[Light Source] --> HV2[Eye Optics Cornea and Lens]
        HV2 --> HV3[Retina Photoreceptors]
        HV3 --> HV4[Optic Nerve]
        HV4 --> HV5[Visual Cortex]
        HV5 --> HV6[Brain Interpretation]
    end
    subgraph ComputerVision[Computer Vision System]
        CV1[Light Source Illumination] --> CV2[Camera Lens Optics]
        CV2 --> CV3[CMOS or CCD Sensor Array]
        CV3 --> CV4[ADC and Storage]
        CV4 --> CV5[CPU or GPU Processor]
        CV5 --> CV6[Algorithm Output Recognition]
    end
```

### 4.3 A Brief History of Computer Vision (Timeline Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[1966 MIT Summer Vision Project] --> H2[1970s Low Level Edge and Region Detection]
    H2 --> H3[1980s Marr Pyramid Theory and 3D Reconstruction]
    H3 --> H4[1990s Statistical Methods Eigenfaces SVMs]
    H4 --> H5[2000s Feature Based SIFT HOG Haar Cascades]
    H5 --> H6[2012 AlexNet Deep Learning Revolution]
    H6 --> H7[2017 Transformer Era ViT and DETR]
    H7 --> H8[2020s Generative Models and Foundation Models]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Historical Milestones (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **1966:** Seymour Papert and Marvin Minsky at MIT launched the "Summer Vision Project", considered the birth of CV.
> - **1982:** David Marr's book *Vision* introduced the three-level paradigm (primal sketch → 2.5D sketch → 3D model).
> - **2001:** Viola-Jones face detection algorithm enabled real-time face detection.
> - **2012:** AlexNet's victory in ImageNet sparked the deep learning revolution in CV.
> - **2017:** Introduction of Vision Transformers (ViT) challenged the dominance of CNNs.

### 4.4 Block Diagram of a Standard Image Processing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IP1[Image Sensor or Storage] --> IP2[Image Acquisition]
    IP2 --> IP3[Preprocessing]
    IP3 --> IP4[Enhancement]
    IP4 --> IP5[Segmentation]
    IP5 --> IP6[Feature Extraction]
    IP6 --> IP7[Image Database for Recognition]
    IP7 --> IP8[Output Display or Decision]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**"Differentiate between Computer Vision and Image Processing. Provide one real-world application of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Image Processing | Computer Vision |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Processing an image to enhance it or extract basic features. Output is often an image. | Enabling machines to interpret and understand the content of images. Output is a decision/label. |
| **Goal** | Improve visual quality or extract low-level features. | Achieve high-level understanding. |
| **Application Example** | Removing noise from a chest X-ray. | Automatically detecting pneumonia from that X-ray. |

**Distribution of Marks (KTU Standard):** Definition difference — **1.5 marks**, Application example — **1.5 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**"Explain the role of sampling and quantization in converting a real-world scene into a digital image."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Sampling** discretizes the spatial domain of the continuous image into an $M \times N$ grid of pixels, governed by $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$.
- **Quantization** discretizes the intensity (amplitude) values into $L = 2^k$ discrete grey levels.
- **Joint effect:** Together they convert a continuous 2D function $f(x, y)$ into a digital matrix suitable for storage and algorithmic processing. **Example:** A 6 MP camera with 8-bit depth yields a 3000 × 2000 image with 256 grey levels per pixel.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For 14-mark questions, KTU evaluators **strictly deduct 1–2 marks** if the student does not:
> 1. Write the **mathematical expression/formula** wherever applicable.
> 2. Provide a **neatly labelled diagram** for any pipeline/process question.
> 3. Use a **comparison table** for "differentiate" sub-questions.
> 4. Mention **real-world applications** to demonstrate engineering relevance.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the three hierarchical levels of Computer Vision. For each level, provide one specific algorithm and its application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Low-Level Vision (Image Processing Stage):**
- Operates on raw pixel data; output is an image.
- **Algorithm:** Canny Edge Detection.
- **Application:** Detecting the outline of a tumour in a CT scan.

**Mid-Level Vision (Feature Extraction Stage):**
- Extracts geometric primitives and segments the image.
- **Algorithm:** Watershed Segmentation.
- **Application:** Separating touching cells in a microscope image.

**High-Level Vision (Recognition Stage):**
- Performs semantic understanding and recognition.
- **Algorithm:** YOLOv8 (You Only Look Once).
- **Application:** Real-time pedestrian detection in autonomous vehicles.

**Diagrammatic Justification:** A hierarchical pyramid diagram (Image → Features → Semantics) must be drawn. *(Refer to SECTION 4.1)*.

**Q. (b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the fundamental steps in a typical Image Processing system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Refer to the block diagram in SECTION 4.4. The explanation must cover:
- **Image Acquisition (1 mark):** Sensor selection, lens, lighting setup.
- **Preprocessing (1 mark):** Noise removal using Gaussian/median filters.
- **Enhancement (1 mark):** Histogram equalization, sharpening.
- **Segmentation (1 mark):** Thresholding, edge-based segmentation.
- **Feature Extraction (1 mark):** SIFT, HOG, LBP descriptors.
- **Recognition and Interpretation (1 mark):** Using ML/DL classifiers.
- **Output Display (1 mark):** Display device or control signal to actuator.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the total memory required (in MB) to store a $4096 \times 2160$ colour image with 8 bits per channel. Assume the standard RGB colour model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $M = 4096$, $N = 2160$, $k = 8$ bits/channel, $C = 3$ (RGB).

**Step 2 — Total bits:**
$$B_{total} = M \times N \times k \times C = 4096 \times 2160 \times 8 \times 3$$

$$B_{total} = 212,336,640 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bytes (÷ 8):**
$$S_{bytes} = 212,336,640 / 8 = 26,542,080 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB (÷ 1024²):**
$$S_{MB} = \dfrac{26,542,080}{1,048,576} \approx 25.31 \text{ MB}$$

**Answer:** The image requires approximately **25.31 MB** of storage.

**Q. (b)** Discuss in detail any **five major applications** of Computer Vision in real-world engineering. For each, mention the specific CV task performed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (any 5 of the following):**

1. **Autonomous Driving (ADAS):** Object detection, lane segmentation, traffic sign recognition using CNNs.
2. **Medical Imaging:** Tumour segmentation, X-ray classification, MRI volumetric analysis.
3. **Industrial Quality Control:** Defect detection on manufacturing lines using template matching and CNNs.
4. **Agricultural Monitoring (KTU Kerala Context):** Coconut palm disease detection from drone imagery using transfer learning.
5. **Biometric Authentication:** Face recognition (FaceNet, ArcFace), fingerprint verification.
6. **Augmented Reality / Virtual Reality:** SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), 6-DoF pose estimation.
7. **Surveillance and Security:** Anomaly detection, crowd density estimation, action recognition.
8. **Satellite Image Analysis:** Land cover classification, flood mapping (highly relevant for Kerala floods).

*(Each application = 1.4 marks: 1 mark for the application description + 0.4 mark for the CV task.)*

---

### 5.3 Major Challenges in Computer Vision (High-Yield for KTU 2024)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "Challenges in CV" is a **favourite Part A and short-note question**. Memorize the 6 challenges below with one-line explanations.

1. **Viewpoint Variation:** The same 3D object looks different from different camera angles.
2. **Illumination Changes:** Pixel intensities vary drastically with lighting conditions (e.g., Kerala's strong sunlight vs. monsoon overcast).
3. **Scale Variation:** Objects appear at different sizes based on their distance from the camera.
4. **Occlusion:** Important parts of the object may be hidden behind other objects.
5. **Background Clutter:** Distinguishing the object of interest from a complex background.
6. **Intra-class Variation:** Different instances of the same class (e.g., different breeds of dogs) look vastly different.
7. **Real-time Processing Constraints:** Many applications (autonomous driving, surveillance) demand millisecond-level latency.
8. **Data Scarcity and Annotation Cost:** Labelled image data is expensive to obtain, especially for medical and satellite domains.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- [x] **Image Formation Equation:** $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ with $0 \leq r \leq 1$ and $i > 0$.
- [x] **Three Levels of CV:** Low-level (image-to-image), Mid-level (image-to-features), High-level (image-to-symbols).
- [x] **Digitization = Sampling + Quantization.** Sampling → spatial; Quantization → intensity.
- [x] **Storage Formula:** $\text{Memory (MB)} = \dfrac{M \times N \times k \times C}{8 \times 1024^2}$.
- [x] **Number of Grey Levels:** $L = 2^k$ (e.g., 8-bit → 256 levels).
- [x] **Nyquist Sampling Rule:** Sampling rate $\geq 2 \times$ highest frequency.
- [x] **CV ≠ IP:** CV outputs **understanding**; IP outputs **enhanced images**.
- [x] **Five Key Applications:** Autonomous driving, medical imaging, industrial inspection, agriculture, surveillance.
- [x] **Six Major Challenges:** Viewpoint, illumination, scale, occlusion, clutter, intra-class variation.
- [x] **Historical Milestones:** 1966 (MIT Project), 1982 (Marr's Vision), 2012 (AlexNet/Deep Learning revolution).
- [x] **Key Terms:** Pixel, Resolution, Bit-depth, RGB, Grayscale, Histogram (refer Module 2).
- [x] **Photometric Units:** Intensity in candela per square metre (cd/m²); irradiance in W/m².

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Image Formation and Pinhole Camera Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Image Formation and Pinhole Camera Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Image Formation

**Image formation** is the geometric and physical process by which a three-dimensional (3D) scene in the physical world is optically projected onto a two-dimensional (2D) sensing surface (film, CCD, CMOS) to produce a measurable irradiance pattern. In the KTU 2024 Computer Vision syllabus, this process is treated primarily as a **geometric projection problem** governed by the laws of **classical (ray) optics**, ignoring wave phenomena such as diffraction in the introductory formulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The pinhole (or central projection) model assumes light travels in straight rays, all rays from a scene point pass through a single common point called the **optical center** (or **center of projection**), and an image is formed on an image plane at a distance $f$ (the focal length) from this center.

## 1.2 The Pinhole Camera — Intuition

**Conceptual Analogy (Camera Obscura):** Imagine a completely dark cardboard box. On one face, you poke a single, infinitesimally small hole with a needle. On the opposite inner face, you paste a sheet of white paper. When you point the box toward a bright outdoor scene (a tree, a building), an astonishing thing happens — an **upside-down (inverted) image** of the scene appears on the paper. This is the **pinhole camera** (Latin: *camera obscura*, literally "dark room"). The single hole ensures that each point in the world is mapped to exactly one point on the paper, producing a sharp, focused image. The smaller the hole, the sharper the image (up to a diffraction limit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study pinhole cameras in Computer Vision?** Even though real cameras use **lenses** to gather more light, the pinhole model is the **mathematical backbone** of all modern camera calibration, 3D reconstruction, SLAM, structure-from-motion, and augmented reality. The lens is mathematically modeled as a *refinement* of the pinhole geometry, and almost every KTU exam problem begins by assuming ideal pinhole projection.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light** in vacuum: $c \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$.
- **Refractive index of air**: $n_{air} \approx \mathbf{1.000293}$.
- **Refractive index of typical optical glass**: $n_{glass} \approx \mathbf{1.5}$.
- **Visible spectrum wavelength range**: $\lambda \approx \mathbf{380 \; nm}$ to $\mathbf{750 \; nm}$.
- **Focal length** $f$ in pinhole model: a free design parameter (typically in **millimeters** for cameras, **meters** for astronomy).
- **Field of View (FOV)**: $\theta_{FOV} = 2 \arctan\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$, where $h$ is the sensor height.

## 1.4 Core Concept Map

| Concept | One-line Definition | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Light ray** | A straight line along which light energy propagates. | Foundation of geometric optics. |
| **Optical center (C)** | Common point through which all image-forming rays pass. | Origin of the camera coordinate system. |
| **Image plane** | 2D surface where the image is formed, at distance $f$ from $C$. | The "screen" on which 2D pixels live. |
| **Focal length ($f$)** | Perpendicular distance between the optical center and the image plane. | Controls magnification and FOV. |
| **Principal point ($c$)** | The point where the optical axis pierces the image plane. | Defines the image center $(c_x, c_y)$. |
| **Pixel** | The smallest discrete sensing element on the image plane. | The output units of any digital camera. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Model — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Choose a coordinate system.**
Place the **optical center** $C$ at the origin of a 3D coordinate system called the **camera frame** $(X, Y, Z)$. The $Z$-axis points along the optical axis (the direction the camera is "looking"). The $X$ and $Y$ axes span the image plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this convention?** It dramatically simplifies the projection equation. By placing $C$ at the origin, the world-to-camera transformation becomes a pure rotation + translation, which in turn becomes a $3 \times 4$ matrix.

**Step 2: Define the image plane.**
The image plane is a virtual plane located at $Z = f$ (in front of $C$ if we want an upright, non-inverted image — this is the modern *virtual image plane* convention) or at $Z = -f$ (behind $C$, which gives the physically correct inverted image). Both formulations are equivalent; we use the **front image plane** because it removes the minus sign.

**Step 3: Apply similar triangles for projection.**
For a 3D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ with $Z > f$, the line from $C$ to $P$ intersects the image plane at point $p = (x, y, f)$. By the **intercept theorem** (Thales' theorem), the triangles $(C, 0, 0)$–$(X, Y, Z)$ and $(C, 0, 0)$–$(x, y, f)$ are similar. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{x}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z}, \qquad \dfrac{y}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z}$$

Solving for image coordinates:

$$x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \qquad y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 4: Generalize to homogeneous coordinates.**
To unify the projective (non-linear) division by $Z$ into a single linear matrix multiplication, we switch to **homogeneous coordinates** by appending a $1$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\longrightarrow\; \begin{bmatrix} fX \\ fY \\ Z \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \text{then dehomogenize: } (x, y) = \left( \dfrac{fX}{Z}, \dfrac{fY}{Z} \right)$$

**Step 5: Introduce the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$.**
A real sensor has pixels of size $(s_x, s_y)$ in mm/pixel and a principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$. The **camera intrinsic matrix** is:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $f_x = f/s_x$ and $f_y = f/s_y$ are focal lengths expressed in *pixel units*, and $s$ is the **skew parameter** (zero for most modern cameras).

**Step 6: Introduce the extrinsic matrix $[\mathbf{R} \,|\, \mathbf{t}]$.**
Since the world frame and camera frame generally do not coincide, we apply a rigid-body transformation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{R} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t}$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix and $\mathbf{t}$ is a $3 \times 1$ translation vector. Both encode the camera's **pose** with respect to the world.

**Step 7: The complete pinhole projection equation.**

$$\boxed{\;s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\;}$$

where $(u, v)$ are the final **pixel coordinates** and $s$ is the homogeneous scale factor. This single equation is the **most-asked equation in KTU Computer Vision exams**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \; y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | Pinhole projection (camera frame) | pixels or mm |
| 2 | $\begin{bmatrix} fX \\ fY \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$ | Homogeneous projection (camera frame) | mixed |
| 3 | $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic matrix | pixels |
| 4 | $f_x = f / s_x, \; f_y = f / s_y$ | Focal length in pixel units | pixels |
| 5 | $\theta_{FOV} = 2\arctan\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$ | Vertical field of view | radians |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$ | Thin lens equation (Gaussian optics) | 1/length |
| 7 | $N = f / D$ | F-number of a lens | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\mathbf{P}_{cam} = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_{world} + \mathbf{t}$ | World → camera (extrinsic) | mixed |
| 9 | $s [u, v, 1]^T = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{P}_w$ | Full pinhole projection | pixels |
| 10 | $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ | Camera projection matrix ($3 \times 4$) | pixels |
| 11 | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ | Essential matrix (epipolar geometry) | mixed |
| 12 | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ | Fundamental matrix | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In formula sheets, the **vertical pipe** `|` inside an expression can break Markdown table rendering. Always use `\vert` (e.g., $\mathbf{R} \vert \mathbf{t}$) or `\mid` to denote the augmentation of matrices $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ inside the projection matrix.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The pinhole model is the **lingua franca** of:
- **Photogrammetry** (3D mapping from aerial imagery),
- **Visual SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping in robots and autonomous vehicles),
- **Structure-from-Motion (SfM)** in computational photography,
- **Augmented Reality (AR)** head-mounted displays,
- **Medical imaging** (endoscope calibration, microscope geometry).

> [!NOTE]
> The pinhole model is **first-order accurate**. Real lenses add **radial distortion** ($k_1, k_2, k_3$) and **tangential distortion** ($p_1, p_2$), which are addressed in later modules. KTU 2024 Module 1 focuses on the **undistorted** linear pinhole projection only.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pinhole Perspective Projection from Similar Triangles

**Statement:** For a 3D scene point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ expressed in the camera coordinate frame, the image plane projection is $(x, y) = \left( fX/Z, \; fY/Z \right)$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: Setup.**
Consider the camera optical center $C$ at the origin of the camera frame, and let the image plane be the plane $Z = f$ (positive focal length, front image plane convention). The 3D point $P$ lies on the ray that makes an angle with the optical axis.

**Step 2: Parameterize the ray.**
A point on the line from $C = (0,0,0)$ to $P = (X, Y, Z)$ is given by $\lambda P = (\lambda X, \lambda Y, \lambda Z)$ for some scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 3: Impose the image-plane constraint.**
This point lies on the image plane when its $Z$-coordinate equals $f$:

$$\lambda Z = f \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = \dfrac{f}{Z}$$

**Step 4: Read off the image coordinates.**
The $X$ and $Y$ coordinates of the image point $p$ are therefore:

$$x = \lambda X = \dfrac{f}{Z} \cdot X = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$$

$$y = \lambda Y = \dfrac{f}{Z} \cdot Y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 5: Express in homogeneous form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} fX \\ fY \\ Z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where the symbol "$\sim$" denotes equality up to a non-zero scalar multiple (i.e., projective equality). This is the **canonical projection matrix** $\mathbf{P}_0$ in the KTU syllabus.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thin Lens Equation (Gaussian Optics)

**Statement:** For a thin lens of focal length $f$, an object at distance $d_o$ from the lens is sharply focused at an image distance $d_i$ on the opposite side, satisfying:

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: Snell's law at a spherical surface.**
At a refracting spherical surface of radius $R$, separating media of refractive indices $n_1$ and $n_2$, the paraxial (small-angle) Snell's law gives:

$$\dfrac{n_1}{s} + \dfrac{n_2}{s'} = \dfrac{n_2 - n_1}{R}$$

where $s$ is the object distance and $s'$ is the image distance (sign convention: distances measured in the direction of light propagation are positive).

**Step 2: Apply to the first refracting surface of the lens.**
For the first surface, $n_1 = n_{air}$, $n_2 = n_{glass}$, radius $= R_1$:

$$\dfrac{n_{air}}{s} + \dfrac{n_{glass}}{s'_1} = \dfrac{n_{glass} - n_{air}}{R_1}$$

**Step 3: Apply to the second refracting surface.**
Light inside the lens travels from the first image (at distance $s'_1$ from surface 1) to surface 2. The new object distance for surface 2 is $s_2 = t - s'_1$ where $t$ is the lens thickness. For a *thin* lens, $t \to 0$, so $s_2 \approx -s'_1$. With $n_1 = n_{glass}$, $n_2 = n_{air}$, radius $= R_2$:

$$-\dfrac{n_{glass}}{s'_1} + \dfrac{n_{air}}{s'} = \dfrac{n_{air} - n_{glass}}{R_2}$$

**Step 4: Add the two equations.**
Adding the equations from Step 2 and Step 3, the $n_{glass}/s'_1$ terms cancel:

$$\dfrac{n_{air}}{s} + \dfrac{n_{air}}{s'} = (n_{glass} - n_{air})\left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

**Step 5: Recognize the lensmaker's equation.**
Dividing both sides by $n_{air}$ and using the **lensmaker's formula** $\dfrac{1}{f} = (n_{rel} - 1)\left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$ where $n_{rel} = n_{glass}/n_{air}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{s} + \dfrac{1}{s'} = \dfrac{1}{f}$$

Renaming $s = d_o$ and $s' = d_i$:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}\;}$$

**Step 6: Limiting cases (intuition).**
- If $d_o \to \infty$ (object at infinity), then $d_i = f$ — the image forms at the focal plane. This is the **astronomical / landscape setting**.
- If $d_o = 2f$, then $d_i = 2f$ — symmetric, **magnification = $-1$**.
- If $d_o = f$, then $d_i \to \infty$ — the image is at infinity, the rays emerge parallel; this is the **collimation setting** used in projectors.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Full Projection Matrix $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$

**Statement:** A 3D world point $\mathbf{P}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$ in homogeneous coordinates projects to pixel $\mathbf{p} = (u, v, 1)^T$ via $\mathbf{p} \sim \mathbf{K} \mathbf{P}_{cam}$ where $\mathbf{P}_{cam} = [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{P}_w$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: World → Camera (extrinsic).**
Apply rotation $\mathbf{R}$ (3×3) and translation $\mathbf{t}$ (3×1):

$$\mathbf{P}_{cam} = \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 1 establishes the relationship between the world coordinate frame and the camera coordinate frame. Since both are Euclidean 3D spaces, the transformation between them must be a rigid body motion, which consists of a rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and a translation $\mathbf{t}$. The 4×4 matrix form is the standard homogeneous representation that concatenates the rotation and translation into a single matrix product, which preserves the linear algebraic structure.

**Step 2: Project onto normalized image plane (intrinsic-free).**
By the pinhole projection of Derivation 1:

$$x_n = \dfrac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_n = \dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

Step 2 performs the perspective divide. The "normalized image plane" is a virtual plane at $Z = 1$ in the camera frame. It is a useful intermediate coordinate system because it factors out the focal length and pixel size; the resulting coordinates are dimensionless and lie in the range $(-1, +1)$ for a unit square sensor.

**Step 3: Apply pixel scaling and offset (intrinsic).**
Pixel coordinates are obtained by scaling $(x_n, y_n)$ by the pixel pitches $(f/s_x, f/s_y)$ and translating by the principal point $(c_x, c_y)$:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_n + s \cdot y_n + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \cdot y_n + c_y$$

Step 3 introduces the physical pixel geometry. A real CMOS/CCD sensor is a discrete array of square (or nearly square) photosites. The factor $f_x = f / s_x$ converts the dimensionless normalized coordinate into pixel units, and $c_x, c_y$ account for the fact that the principal point (where the optical axis hits the sensor) is generally not exactly at the geometric center of the image array. The skew parameter $s$ accounts for non-rectangular pixels; it is zero in modern sensors.

**Step 4: Write as a single matrix equation.**

$$\begin{aligned} s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c / Z_c \\ Y_c / Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

Step 4 is the formal composition. The "s" in front of the pixel vector is the homogeneous scale factor — since homogeneous coordinates are only defined up to a non-zero scalar, dehomogenization by dividing by the third element recovers the affine pixel coordinates.

**Step 5: Define the camera projection matrix.**

$$\mathbf{M}_{3 \times 4} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} m_{11} & m_{12} & m_{13} & m_{14} \\ m_{21} & m_{22} & m_{23} & m_{24} \\ m_{31} & m_{32} & m_{33} & m_{34} \end{bmatrix}$$

This $\mathbf{M}$ has **11 degrees of freedom** (5 intrinsic + 6 extrinsic) up to an overall scale. It is the cornerstone of **camera calibration** in KTU Module 1 lab work.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

**Problem:** A camera has $f = 50 \; mm$, sensor size $36 \times 24 \; mm$, image resolution $6000 \times 4000$ pixels, principal point at the center, and zero skew. A 3D point at world coordinates $(2, 3, 10)$ m is in the camera frame (no rotation/translation needed). Find the pixel coordinates.

**Solution:**

**Step A: Pixel pitch.**

$$s_x = \dfrac{36}{6000} = 0.006 \; \text{mm/pixel}, \qquad s_y = \dfrac{24}{4000} = 0.006 \; \text{mm/pixel}$$

**Step B: Focal length in pixels.**

$$f_x = f_y = \dfrac{50}{0.006} = 8333.33 \; \text{pixels}$$

**Step C: Principal point.**

$$c_x = 6000/2 = 3000, \qquad c_y = 4000/2 = 2000$$

**Step D: Intrinsic matrix.**

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 8333.33 & 0 & 3000 \\ 0 & 8333.33 & 2000 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step E: Projection.**

$$\begin{aligned} u &= 8333.33 \cdot \dfrac{2}{10} + 3000 = 1666.67 + 3000 = 4666.67 \; \text{pixels} \\[4pt] v &= 8333.33 \cdot \dfrac{3}{10} + 2000 = 2500 + 2000 = 4500 \; \text{pixels} \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\mathbf{p} = (4666.67, \; 4500)$ pixels — well within the $6000 \times 4000$ sensor. ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Pinhole Camera Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World 3D Point P at X, Y, Z] --> B[Ray from P to Optical Center C]
    C[Optical Center C at origin of camera frame] --> D[Image plane at Z equals f]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Image point p at x equals fX over Z, y equals fY over Z]
    E --> F[Apply Intrinsic Matrix K for pixel scaling]
    F --> G[Final pixel coordinates u, v]
    H[Extrinsic R and t: world to camera transform] --> B
    A --> H
    I[Pixel pitch s_x and s_y] --> F
    J[Principal point c_x, c_y] --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:** A 3D world point $P$ is first transformed from world coordinates to camera coordinates by the extrinsic block ($\mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{t}$). Then the ray from $P$ through the optical center $C$ is intersected with the image plane at $Z = f$ to obtain the perspective projection. Finally, the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ converts metric image coordinates to pixel coordinates using the pixel pitch and the principal point.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Image Formation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph worldBlock["World Stage"]
        direction TB
        worldPoint["World Point Pw in Xw, Yw, Zw"]
        worldFrame["World Coordinate Frame"]
        worldPoint --> worldFrame
    end
    subgraph extrinsicBlock["Extrinsic Transformation Block"]
        direction TB
        rotationNode["Rotation Matrix R 3x3"]
        translationNode["Translation Vector t 3x1"]
        rototransNode["Concatenated Matrix R and t"]
        rotationNode --> rototransNode
        translationNode --> rototransNode
    end
    subgraph cameraBlock["Camera Stage"]
        direction TB
        opticalCenter["Optical Center C at 0,0,0"]
        imagePlane["Image Plane at Z equals f"]
        projectionNode["Perspective Divide by Z"]
        opticalCenter --> imagePlane
        imagePlane --> projectionNode
    end
    subgraph intrinsicBlock["Intrinsic Transformation Block"]
        direction TB
        focalNode["Focal Length fx, fy in pixels"]
        principalNode["Principal Point cx, cy"]
        skewNode["Skew Parameter s"]
        kMatrixNode["Intrinsic Matrix K 3x3"]
        focalNode --> kMatrixNode
        principalNode --> kMatrixNode
        skewNode --> kMatrixNode
    end
    subgraph outputBlock["Output Stage"]
        direction TB
        pixelNode["Pixel Coordinates u, v"]
        imageArrayNode["Digital Image Array I of u, v"]
        pixelNode --> imageArrayNode
    end
    worldFrame --> rototransNode
    rototransNode --> projectionNode
    projectionNode --> kMatrixNode
    kMatrixNode --> pixelNode
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pipeline is divided into four functional stages — World, Extrinsic, Camera (geometric projection), Intrinsic, and Output. Each stage is mathematically a matrix multiplication, and the overall pipeline reduces to the single $3 \times 4$ projection matrix $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Field of View vs. Focal Length Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Focal Length f] --> B[Small f: Wide FOV, e.g., 24mm lens]
    A --> C[Medium f: Normal FOV, e.g., 50mm lens]
    A --> D[Large f: Narrow FOV, e.g., 200mm telephoto]
    B --> E[Large scene area captured, small objects]
    C --> F[Natural perspective, 46 degree FOV]
    D --> G[Small scene area, large magnification]
    H[Sensor size h] --> I[FOV depends on f and h]
    I --> B
    I --> C
    I --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:** The field of view is jointly controlled by the focal length and the sensor size. A wide-angle lens (small $f$) captures a large angular swath of the scene, while a telephoto lens (large $f$) zooms into a small angular region. The formula $\theta_{FOV} = 2\arctan(h / (2f))$ governs the trade-off.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the pinhole camera model. List its main assumptions and state the projection equation relating a 3D world point to its 2D image point.**

**Model Answer:**

The pinhole camera model (also called the **central projection model**) describes a camera as an idealized device in which all light rays from a scene pass through a single infinitesimal point called the **optical center** $C$, before striking an **image plane** located at a distance $f$ (the **focal length**) from $C$.

**Main Assumptions:**

1. Light travels in straight lines (geometric optics assumption).
2. The aperture is infinitesimally small — no diffraction effects.
3. All rays from a scene point converge exactly at the optical center.
4. The image plane is planar and perpendicular to the optical axis.
5. The lens is free from all optical aberrations (no distortion).

**Projection Equation (in camera frame):**

For a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ with $Z > f$, the image-plane projection is $(x, y) = \left( fX/Z, \; fY/Z \right)$. In matrix form, using homogeneous coordinates:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for assumptions, 1 for equation]**

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What are intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters? Differentiate between them with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Intrinsic Parameters** describe the **internal optical and geometric properties** of the camera itself, independent of where the camera is placed in the world. They are constants for a given camera (after manufacturing). The intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ contains:

- $f_x, f_y$ — focal length in pixel units.
- $c_x, c_y$ — coordinates of the **principal point** (where the optical axis meets the image plane).
- $s$ — **skew parameter** (zero for most modern sensors).

**Extrinsic Parameters** describe the **pose** (position and orientation) of the camera in the world coordinate frame. They consist of:

- A $3 \times 3$ **rotation matrix** $\mathbf{R}$ (3 degrees of freedom — e.g., roll, pitch, yaw).
- A $3 \times 1$ **translation vector** $\mathbf{t}$ (3 degrees of freedom — $X, Y, Z$ position of $C$).

**Key Difference:**

| Aspect | Intrinsic | Extrinsic |
|---|---|---|
| Depends on camera pose? | No | Yes |
| Changes if you move the camera? | No | Yes |
| Number of DOF | 5 | 6 |
| Calibrated by | Zhang's method, geometric calibration | Solving PnP, SLAM, GPS+IMU |

**[3 marks: 1 each for intrinsic and extrinsic definitions, 1 for differentiation with example]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign Convention Trap:** When using the front image plane, students often forget the *virtual* image is upright. When using the rear image plane (physically correct), they forget the *negative sign* in $x = -fX/Z$. Read the question carefully and state your convention explicitly.
> - **Order of Multiplication:** Matrix multiplication is **not commutative**. $\mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is **NOT** equal to $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]\mathbf{K}$. The correct order is **first** extrinsics (world → camera), **then** intrinsics (camera metric → pixel). Getting this backwards costs **at least 2 marks**.
> - **Units of Focal Length:** A focal length in mm is **not** the same as a focal length in pixels. Always convert via $f_x = f / s_x$ before plugging into $\mathbf{K}$.
> - **Dehomogenization:** Many students write the homogeneous pixel vector as the answer. Always divide by the third (scale) element to give affine $(u, v)$ coordinates.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, derive the **pinhole camera projection equations** in the camera coordinate frame. Clearly state the assumptions of the pinhole model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A camera has focal length $f = 35 \; mm$, a $24 \times 16 \; mm$ sensor with $4000 \times 2667$ pixel resolution. The principal point is at the center of the sensor and skew is zero. A 3D world point is at position $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c) = (0.5, \; -0.2, \; 5.0)$ meters in the camera frame. Compute the pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ of the projected point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** See **Section 3.1** above. Key steps: place optical center at origin → image plane at $Z = f$ → parameterize ray as $\lambda P$ → impose $Z$-constraint $\lambda = f/Z$ → obtain $x = fX/Z$ and $y = fY/Z$. Express in homogeneous form. Mention all five assumptions explicitly (see Part A Q1). Draw a 2D cross-section diagram showing the optical center, the inverted image, the object, and the similar triangles. **[7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 2 for assumptions]**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pixel pitch.**

$$s_x = \dfrac{24 \; \text{mm}}{4000 \; \text{px}} = 0.006 \; \text{mm/px}, \qquad s_y = \dfrac{16 \; \text{mm}}{2667 \; \text{px}} \approx 0.006 \; \text{mm/px}$$

**Step 2 — Focal length in pixels.**

$$f_x = \dfrac{f}{s_x} = \dfrac{35}{0.006} = 5833.33 \; \text{px}, \qquad f_y = \dfrac{f}{s_y} = \dfrac{35}{0.006} = 5833.33 \; \text{px}$$

**Step 3 — Principal point.**

$$c_x = 4000/2 = 2000, \qquad c_y = 2667/2 = 1333.5$$

**Step 4 — Intrinsic matrix.**

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 5833.33 & 0 & 2000 \\ 0 & 5833.33 & 1333.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Normalized projection.**

$$x_n = \dfrac{X_c}{Z_c} = \dfrac{0.5}{5.0} = 0.10, \qquad y_n = \dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c} = \dfrac{-0.2}{5.0} = -0.04$$

**Step 6 — Pixel coordinates.**

$$u = f_x \cdot x_n + c_x = 5833.33 \times 0.10 + 2000 = 583.33 + 2000 = 2583.33 \; \text{px}$$

$$v = f_y \cdot y_n + c_y = 5833.33 \times (-0.04) + 1333.5 = -233.33 + 1333.5 = 1100.17 \; \text{px}$$

**Final Answer:** $\mathbf{p} = (2583.33, \; 1100.17)$ pixels. The negative $Y_c$ yields $v < c_y$, as expected (below the principal axis). ✓

**[7 marks: 1 each for Steps 1–4, 1 for Step 5, 1 for Step 6, 1 for final verification]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the **thin lens equation** $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$ starting from Snell's law at a spherical refracting surface. Discuss its limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **full pinhole projection equation** $s \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{P}_w$, compute the pixel coordinates of a 3D world point $\mathbf{P}_w = (1, 2, 3, 1)^T$ given the following camera calibration:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Show all intermediate steps and verify your result is inside a $640 \times 480$ image. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thin Lens Derivation:** See **Section 3.2** above. Key steps: Snell's law at surface 1 → Snell's law at surface 2 → thin-lens limit $t \to 0$ → add the two equations → recognize lensmaker's formula → obtain $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$. Limiting cases to mention explicitly:

- $d_o \to \infty$: $d_i = f$ (image at focal plane).
- $d_o = f$: $d_i \to \infty$ (collimated output).
- $d_o = 2f$: $d_i = 2f$ and magnification $m = -1$.
- $d_o \to 0$: $d_i \to 0$ (object on lens surface).

**[7 marks: 5 for derivation, 2 for limiting cases]**

**(b) Full Projection Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{P}_w$.**

First, $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_{w,xyz}$:

$$\mathbf{R} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 1 + (-1) \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 3 \\ 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 2 + (-1) \cdot 3 \\ 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then add $\mathbf{t}$:

$$\mathbf{P}_{cam} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 1 applies the extrinsic transformation to convert the point from world coordinates to camera coordinates. The result shows that in the camera frame, the point lies at $(-2, -3, 2)$.

**Step 2 — Perspective divide.**

$$x_n = \dfrac{-2}{2} = -1, \qquad y_n = \dfrac{-3}{2} = -1.5$$

Step 2 is the nonlinear projection onto the normalized image plane. The point lies to the left of and below the principal point in the camera frame.

**Step 3 — Apply intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$.**

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 800(-1) + 0 + 320 \\ 0 + 800(-1.5) + 240 \\ 0 + 0 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -480 \\ -960 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 3 applies the intrinsic matrix to convert the normalized image coordinates into homogeneous pixel coordinates. The computation is a direct matrix-vector product.

**Step 4 — Dehomogenize.**

Since the third component is already $1$, we have directly:

$$u = -480, \qquad v = -960$$

Step 4 recognizes that the homogeneous scale factor is $s = 1$, so no division is needed.

**Step 5 — Verify against image bounds.**

The image is $640 \times 480$ pixels, with valid ranges $u \in [0, 639]$ and $v \in [0, 479]$. The computed pixel $(u, v) = (-480, -960)$ lies **outside** the image — the point is **not visible** in this camera. This makes geometric sense: the camera is looking in the $-Y$ direction (based on $\mathbf{R}$), and the world point is at $Y_w = 2$, which is behind the camera's effective viewing frustum after the rotation.

**Final Answer:** $(u, v) = (-480, -960)$ — **the point is outside the $640 \times 480$ image**, illustrating that pinhole projection can yield physically invalid (off-screen) pixel coordinates that must be clipped during rendering.

**[7 marks: 2 for Step 1, 1 for Step 2, 1 for Step 3, 1 for Step 4, 2 for Step 5 verification]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire):**

- ⭐ The **pinhole camera** is a central projection model: every 3D point $P$ projects to image point $p$ along a ray through the **optical center** $C$.
- ⭐ The fundamental projection equation in the camera frame is $x = fX/Z, \; y = fY/Z$.
- ⭐ The **intrinsic matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ is a $3 \times 3$ upper-triangular matrix with 5 DOF: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, s$.
- ⭐ The **extrinsic matrix** $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is a $3 \times 4$ matrix with 6 DOF: 3 rotations + 3 translations.
- ⭐ The **full projection matrix** $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is $3 \times 4$ with 11 DOF (up to scale).
- ⭐ Always dehomogenize by dividing the homogeneous pixel vector by its third element to get $(u, v)$.
- ⭐ The **thin lens equation** $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$ is the foundation of all lens-based optics.
- ⭐ The **field of view** depends on **both** focal length and sensor size: $\theta_{FOV} = 2\arctan(h / (2f))$.
- ⭐ Pinhole model is **first-order** — it ignores lens distortion, diffraction, and depth-of-field effects.
- ⭐ Units: $f$ in mm, $s_x, s_y$ in mm/pixel, $f_x = f/s_x$ in pixels, $c_x, c_y$ in pixels.
- ⭐ A pixel with $u < 0$ or $u \geq W$ or $v < 0$ or $v \geq H$ is **off-screen** — discard it.
- ⭐ In KTU exams, always state the **sign convention** for the image plane (front vs. rear) explicitly.
- ⭐ The pinhole projection is **non-invertible** from a single image: a single 2D point corresponds to an entire **ray** in 3D (the well-known depth ambiguity).
- ⭐ **Camera calibration** estimates $\mathbf{K}$ and the distortion coefficients using known calibration targets (e.g., a checkerboard) and methods such as **Zhang's plane-based calibration** or **Tsai's two-stage method** (covered in later modules).
- ⭐ The pinhole model underpins **epipolar geometry** (essential matrix $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ and fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Representation (Grayscale, Color Spaces) and Digital Image Properties (Resolution, Bit Depth)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Representation (Grayscale, Color Spaces) and Digital Image Properties (Resolution, Bit Depth)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Digital Image?

A **digital image** is a two-dimensional discrete function $f(x, y)$ that has been digitized in both **spatial coordinates** and **brightness (intensity)**. The digital image is composed of a finite number of elements called **pixels** (picture elements), each with a specific location $(x, y)$ and intensity value $f$.

For an image of size $M \times N$:
- $M$ = number of rows (height in pixels)
- $N$ = number of columns (width in pixels)
- $f(x, y)$ is a discrete intensity value at coordinate $(x, y)$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Gonzalez & Woods Standard):**
> A digital image can be represented as a 2D matrix of size $M \times N$ where each element represents the brightness/intensity of the image at that spatial location. The complete digital image is a discrete sampling of a continuous visual scene.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a digital image like a **mosaic artwork** 🖼️ made of tiny square tiles:
- Each **tile = 1 pixel** (the smallest indivisible unit)
- The **total number of tiles** in horizontal × vertical = **spatial resolution**
- The **color depth** of paint available in each tile = **bit depth**
- A "256-shade grayscale paint set" means 8-bit grayscale
- A "full RGB paint set" means 24-bit color (millions of colors)

**Real-world analogy:** Imagine a classroom seating chart. Each student (pixel) sits in a specific seat $(x, y)$. Each student wears a shirt of a particular color (intensity). The full chart is the digital image. A 4K TV has more "students" arranged densely (high resolution) wearing finely graded colors (high bit depth).

### 1.3 Grayscale Image Representation

A **grayscale image** uses a single intensity channel. The intensity ranges from:
- $0$ (pure black) to $L-1$ (pure white)
- For $k$-bit images, the number of gray levels $L = 2^k$

Common grayscale bit depths:
- **1-bit:** Binary image (black & white only), $L = 2$
- **8-bit:** Standard grayscale, $L = 256$ levels
- **12-bit/16-bit:** Medical/scientific imaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In KTU 2024 scheme, the standard grayscale assumption is **8 bits per pixel** unless otherwise specified. A grayscale image is stored as a 2D matrix $I \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N}$ of values in $[0, 255]$.

### 1.4 Color Image Representation

A **color image** typically uses multiple channels. The most common representation is **RGB (Red, Green, Blue)** where each pixel stores 3 values, one per channel. The image is stored as a 3D tensor $I \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N \times 3}$.

**Common Color Spaces used in Computer Vision:**

| Color Space | Channels | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **RGB** | R, G, B | Display, image sensors, basic CV |
| **HSV / HSI** | Hue, Saturation, Value/Intensity | Color-based segmentation, lighting-invariant analysis |
| **YCbCr** | Y (luma), Cb, Cr (chroma) | JPEG compression, TV broadcasting |
| **Lab (CIELAB)** | L (lightness), a, b | Perceptual uniformity, color difference |
| **CMYK** | Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key (black) | Printing industry |
| **Grayscale** | Single intensity | Edge detection, preprocessing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Hue:** Hue is the dominant color wavelength (0°–360°), Saturation represents color purity (0 = gray, 1 = pure), and Value/Intensity represents brightness.

### 1.5 Digital Image Properties

#### A. Resolution
**Resolution** is the fineness of detail an image holds. There are three types:

1. **Spatial Resolution** — number of pixels per unit area (e.g., $1920 \times 1080$)
2. **Intensity (Gray-level) Resolution** — smallest discernible change in intensity, controlled by **bit depth** $k$
3. **Temporal Resolution** — number of frames per second (video)

#### B. Bit Depth
**Bit depth** $k$ is the number of bits used to represent the intensity of each pixel. It determines:
- The number of distinct intensity levels: $L = 2^k$
- The dynamic range of the image
- The color fidelity

For an RGB color image with 8 bits per channel: total bit depth = **24 bits per pixel**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** More bits = smoother gradients, less banding, better dynamic range, but **larger file size** and **slower processing**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why and How Digital Images are Structured

**Why discretize?** Continuous analog images (from cameras, sensors) must be sampled to be processed by digital computers. Two key operations occur:

1. **Sampling** — discretizes spatial coordinates $(x, y)$
2. **Quantization** — discretizes intensity values $f(x, y)$

**How it works:**
- The 2D image plane is divided into a grid of $M \times N$ cells
- Each cell has a single intensity value (average of light falling on it)
- The intensity is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^k$ discrete levels
- Result: a 2D matrix of integers

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Image Properties

**A. Spatial Resolution (M × N):**
- Determines **detail/sharpness** of the image
- More pixels = smaller pixel size (given fixed physical area) = finer detail
- Common standards: VGA $(640 \times 480)$, HD $(1280 \times 720)$, FHD $(1920 \times 1080)$, 4K UHD $(3840 \times 2160)$, 8K $(7680 \times 4320)$

**B. Intensity Resolution (k bits):**
- Determines **smoothness of intensity transitions**
- Low $k$ (e.g., 2 bits) → "false contouring" artifacts (visible bands)
- High $k$ (e.g., 16 bits) → smooth gradients, essential for medical/scientific imaging
- The human eye can discriminate ~32–64 gray shades under good conditions (so $k = 6$ is often visually sufficient for grayscale display)

**C. Pixel Density (PPI/DPI):**
- **PPI** = Pixels Per Inch (display)
- **DPI** = Dots Per Inch (print)
- Higher PPI = sharper display at given viewing distance

**D. Image Storage:**
- Each grayscale pixel: $k$ bits
- Each RGB pixel: $3k$ bits (typically 24 bits = 3 bytes)
- Total file size scales linearly with $M$, $N$, $k$, and channels $C$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of gray levels | $L = 2^k$ | dimensionless | $k$ = bit depth |
| 2 | Grayscale image bits | $B_{\text{gray}} = M \cdot N \cdot k$ | bits | 1 channel |
| 3 | RGB image bits | $B_{\text{RGB}} = M \cdot N \cdot 3k$ | bits | 3 channels |
| 4 | Image file size (bytes) | $\text{Size} = \dfrac{M \cdot N \cdot C \cdot k}{8}$ | bytes | $C$ = channels |
| 5 | PPI (Pixel Density) | $\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\text{Res}_x \text{ (pixels)}}{\text{Width (inches)}}$ | pixels/inch | Display |
| 6 | Total pixel count | $N_{\text{px}} = M \times N$ | pixels | Resolution |
| 7 | Megapixels | $\text{MP} = \dfrac{M \times N}{10^6}$ | MP | Camera metric |
| 8 | Aspect ratio | $\text{AR} = M : N$ | ratio | e.g., 16:9 |
| 9 | Storage w/ compression | $S = \dfrac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8 \cdot \text{CR}}$ | bytes | CR = compression ratio |
| 10 | RGB to Grayscale (luminance) | $Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B$ | intensity | ITU-R BT.601 |
| 11 | RGB to Grayscale (perceptual) | $Y = 0.2126 R + 0.7152 G + 0.0722 B$ | intensity | ITU-R BT.709 |
| 12 | Normalized intensity | $r = R / (R+G+B)$ | $[0,1]$ | Chromaticity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the pipe symbol `|` inside tables.** For absolute value in formulas inside the table above, none were needed. When writing math outside the table, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to prevent markdown table-breaking.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** 12–16 bit depth preserves diagnostic detail; spatial resolution critical for tumor detection.
- **Satellite Imaging:** Multi-spectral channels (RGB + IR) used in remote sensing; per-channel bit depth up to 16 bits.
- **Surveillance:** Lower spatial resolution acceptable; temporal resolution (frame rate) is paramount.
- **AR/VR Displays:** High PPI (>500) required at close viewing distance; 10-bit color (HDR) increasingly standard.
- **Self-driving Cars:** Multiple color spaces used (RGB for display, HSV for traffic light detection, YCbCr for compression).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit Depth vs Number of Gray Levels

We start with the fundamental definition: with $k$ bits, we can represent $2^k$ unique binary patterns.

**Step 1:** Each bit can be 0 or 1, giving 2 possibilities per bit.

**Step 2:** For $k$ independent bits, the total number of unique patterns is:

$$
N_{\text{patterns}} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots \text{ (k times)} = 2^k
$$

**Step 3:** Mapping these patterns to intensity values 0, 1, 2, ..., $L-1$:

$$
L = 2^k
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $k$:

$$
k = \log_2(L)
$$

**Step 5:** Worked example — for $L = 256$ (standard grayscale):

$$
k = \log_2(256) = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Image Storage Size

**Step 1:** Define image dimensions $M \times N$ (rows × columns).

**Step 2:** Total number of pixels:

$$
N_{\text{px}} = M \times N
$$

**Step 3:** Each pixel stores $k$ bits (grayscale) or $C \cdot k$ bits (multichannel, $C$ channels).

**Step 4:** Total bits in the image:

$$
B_{\text{total}} = M \times N \times C \times k
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to bytes (divide by 8):

$$
\text{Size (bytes)} = \dfrac{M \times N \times C \times k}{8}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to kilobytes/megabytes:

$$
\text{Size (MB)} = \dfrac{M \times N \times C \times k}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} = \dfrac{M \times N \times C \times k}{8{,}388{,}608}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU standard):**
For an HD color image: $M = 1080$, $N = 1920$, $C = 3$, $k = 8$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{px}} &= 1080 \times 1920 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels} \\
B_{\text{total}} &= 1080 \times 1920 \times 3 \times 8 = 47{,}765{,}760 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Size (bytes)} &= \dfrac{47{,}765{,}760}{8} = 5{,}970{,}720 \text{ bytes} \approx 5.7 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: RGB to Grayscale (Luminance Method)

The human eye is **most sensitive to green** and least sensitive to blue. Hence, a weighted average is used rather than a simple average.

**Step 1:** Start with normalized RGB values $R, G, B \in [0, 1]$ (or $[0, 255]$ for 8-bit).

**Step 2:** Apply the ITU-R BT.601 luminance weights (legacy SD/HD video standard):

$$
Y = 0.299 \, R + 0.587 \, G + 0.114 \, B
$$

**Step 3:** Verify the weights sum to 1 (preserves intensity scaling):

$$
0.299 + 0.587 + 0.114 = 1.000 \checkmark
$$

**Step 4:** For 8-bit values, scale accordingly:

$$
Y_{8} = 0.299 \, R_{8} + 0.587 \, G_{8} + 0.114 \, B_{8}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Given $R = 200$, $G = 100$, $B = 50$ (8-bit values):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299(200) + 0.587(100) + 0.114(50) \\
  &= 59.8 + 58.7 + 5.7 \\
  &= 124.2 \approx 124
\end{aligned}
$$

This result is **124** on a 0–255 grayscale scale.

### 3.4 Derivation: RGB to HSV Conversion

The HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) model is more intuitive for human color perception.

**Step 1:** Compute Value (max channel):

$$
V = \max(R, G, B)
$$

**Step 2:** Compute minimum channel:

$$
m = \min(R, G, B)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Saturation:

$$
S = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } V = 0 \\ \dfrac{V - m}{V} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Hue. Let $\delta = V - m$:

$$
H = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \delta = 0 \\
60 \cdot \left( \dfrac{G - B}{\delta} \mod 6 \right) & \text{if } V = R \\
60 \cdot \left( \dfrac{B - R}{\delta} + 2 \right) & \text{if } V = G \\
60 \cdot \left( \dfrac{R - G}{\delta} + 4 \right) & \text{if } V = B
\end{cases}
$$

**Result:** $H \in [0°, 360°)$, $S \in [0, 1]$, $V \in [0, 1]$.

### 3.5 Derivation: Pixel Density (PPI)

**Step 1:** Given a display with resolution $\text{Res}_x \times \text{Res}_y$ pixels and a physical diagonal $d_{\text{diag}}$ inches.

**Step 2:** For a 16:9 aspect ratio, the horizontal width is:

$$
W = \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{16^2 + 9^2}} \cdot d_{\text{diag}} = \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{337}} \cdot d_{\text{diag}} \approx 0.8716 \cdot d_{\text{diag}}
$$

**Step 3:** PPI in horizontal direction:

$$
\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\text{Res}_x}{W} = \dfrac{\text{Res}_x}{0.8716 \cdot d_{\text{diag}}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A 24-inch 1920×1080 monitor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 0.8716 \times 24 \approx 20.92 \text{ inches} \\
\text{PPI} &= \dfrac{1920}{20.92} \approx 91.78 \text{ PPI}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Derivation: Effect of Bit Depth on File Size

Compare two color images of size $1920 \times 1080$ at 8-bit vs 16-bit per channel:

**8-bit RGB:**
$$
\text{Size} = \dfrac{1920 \times 1080 \times 3 \times 8}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} \approx 5.93 \text{ MB}
$$

**16-bit RGB:**
$$
\text{Size} = \dfrac{1920 \times 1080 \times 3 \times 16}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} \approx 11.86 \text{ MB}
$$

This shows bit depth is **linearly proportional** to file size — doubling bit depth doubles storage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Image Representation Hierarchy Flow

This diagram shows the complete conceptual hierarchy of digital image representation, from continuous scene to numerical matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Real World Scene] --> B[Sampling]
    A --> C[Quantization]
    B --> D[Discrete Spatial Grid M x N]
    C --> E[Discrete Intensity Levels L = 2^k]
    D --> F[Digital Image Matrix f x,y]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Number of Channels}
    G -->|C = 1| H[Grayscale Image 2D Matrix M x N]
    G -->|C = 3| I[Color Image 3D Tensor M x N x 3]
    G -->|C > 3| J[Multispectral Image M x N x C]
    H --> K[Properties: Resolution, Bit Depth, File Size]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Ready for Computer Vision Processing]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Color Space Conversion Pipeline

This diagram maps the interaction flow when converting and using different color spaces in a typical CV pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input RGB Image M x N x 3] --> A1[Channel Split R G B]
    A1 --> A2[R Channel Matrix]
    A1 --> A3[G Channel Matrix]
    A1 --> A4[B Channel Matrix]
    A2 --> B1[Grayscale via Luminance 0.299 0.587 0.114]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[Single Channel Gray Image M x N]
    A2 --> C1[Max Channel Value V]
    A3 --> C1
    A4 --> C1
    A2 --> C2[Min Channel Value m]
    A3 --> C2
    A4 --> C2
    C1 --> D1[Compute Saturation V minus m over V]
    C2 --> D1
    C1 --> D2[Compute Hue via Sector Detection]
    A2 --> D2
    A3 --> D2
    A4 --> D2
    D1 --> E1[HSV Color Space Output]
    D2 --> E1
    A2 --> F1[Convert RGB to YCbCr]
    A3 --> F1
    A4 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[YCbCr Output for Compression]
    B2 --> G1[Edge Detection Feature Extraction]
    E1 --> G2[Color Based Segmentation]
    F2 --> G3[JPEG Compression Storage]
    G1 --> H[Computer Vision Tasks]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

### 4.3 Pixel Grid Structure with Resolution Visualization

This diagram illustrates how a digital image is structured as a discrete grid with associated properties.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Digital Image Definition] --> P1[Spatial Dimension M rows]
    Start --> P2[Spatial Dimension N columns]
    Start --> P3[Bit Depth k bits per pixel]
    Start --> P4[Number of Channels C]
    P1 --> Total[Total Pixels M times N]
    P2 --> Total
    P3 --> Levels[Grey Levels L equals 2 to the power k]
    P4 --> Storage[Total File Size M times N times C times k bits]
    Total --> Storage
    Levels --> Storage
    Storage --> Output[Image Ready for Processing]
    P3 --> Resolution[Intensity Resolution]
    Total --> SpatialRes[Spatial Resolution M by N]
    SpatialRes --> PPI[Pixel Density PPI per inch]
```

### 4.4 Resolution vs Bit Depth Trade-off (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Image Quality Determinants] --> B[Spatial Resolution M by N]
    A --> C[Bit Depth k]
    A --> D[Compression]
    B --> B1[High M by N equals Sharp Details]
    B --> B2[Low M by N equals Pixelation]
    C --> C1[High k equals Smooth Gradients]
    C --> C2[Low k equals Banding and False Contours]
    D --> D1[Lossy JPEG smaller files]
    D --> D2[Lossless PNG larger files]
    B1 --> Outcome[Optimal Image Quality]
    C1 --> Outcome
    B2 --> Tradeoff[Visual Artifacts]
    C2 --> Tradeoff
    D1 --> Tradeoff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with respect to digital images: (i) Spatial Resolution, (ii) Intensity Resolution, (iii) Bit Depth. How are they related?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Spatial Resolution (1 mark):** Spatial resolution refers to the number of pixels per unit area in a digital image, typically expressed as $M \times N$ (rows × columns). It quantifies the **fineness of detail** that can be represented spatially. Higher spatial resolution = more detail.

**(ii) Intensity Resolution (1 mark):** Intensity resolution is the **smallest discernible change in intensity level** that an image can represent. It is controlled by the bit depth $k$ and is given by the number of discrete gray levels $L = 2^k$.

**(iii) Bit Depth (1 mark):** Bit depth $k$ is the number of bits used to encode the intensity of each pixel. For grayscale: $k$ bits per pixel. For RGB color: $3k$ bits per pixel (typically 24 bits).

**Relationship:** Bit depth $k$ directly determines intensity resolution ($L = 2^k$). Spatial resolution ($M \times N$) and intensity resolution together determine the **total information content** of an image: $B_{\text{total}} = M \times N \times k$ bits (grayscale).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Explain the RGB color model. How is it different from the HSV color model? Why is HSV often preferred in computer vision tasks?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RGB Model (1 mark):** The RGB (Red, Green, Blue) model is an **additive** color model where each color is represented as a combination of three primary channels. Each pixel stores three values $(R, G, B) \in [0, 255]^3$ for 8-bit images. The color space forms a 3D cube.

**HSV Model (1 mark):** The HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) model represents color using three components: **Hue** (the dominant color wavelength, $0°$–$360°$), **Saturation** (color purity, 0 = gray, 1 = pure), and **Value** (brightness, 0 = black, 1 = bright). It is based on human perceptual intuition.

**Why HSV is preferred in CV (1 mark):** HSV **decouples chromaticity (H, S) from intensity (V)**, making it **robust to lighting variations**. For tasks like skin detection, traffic light recognition, and color-based segmentation, HSV is significantly more reliable than RGB. RGB's R, G, B channels are highly correlated with brightness changes, while H and S remain relatively stable under varying illumination.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. There is **internal choice** between two questions.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** For a digital image of size $1024 \times 1024$ pixels with 256 gray levels:
- (i) Calculate the number of bits required to store the image.
- (ii) Determine the intensity resolution.
- (iii) Comment on what happens if bit depth is reduced to 4 bits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A color image of size $800 \times 600$ is stored as an RGB image with 8 bits per channel.
- (i) Calculate the total file size in KB.
- (ii) How many unique colors can this image represent?
- (iii) If the same image is converted to grayscale using the luminance method, calculate the new file size in KB. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $M = 1024$, $N = 1024$, $L = 256$ gray levels.

**(i) Number of bits to store the image (3 marks):**

We know that the number of gray levels $L = 2^k$. So:

$$
k = \log_2(L) = \log_2(256) = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

Total bits to store the image:

$$
B_{\text{total}} = M \times N \times k
$$

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 1024 \times 1024 \times 8
$$

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bits} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \approx 1 \text{ MB}
$$

**(ii) Intensity resolution (2 marks):**

Intensity resolution is the smallest change in intensity that can be represented. For $k = 8$ bits and range $[0, 255]$:

$$
\Delta I = \dfrac{255 - 0}{L - 1} = \dfrac{255}{255} = 1 \text{ unit of intensity}
$$

So the intensity resolution is **1 gray level** out of 256 possible values.

**(iii) Effect of reducing bit depth to 4 bits (2 marks):**

If $k = 4$, the new number of gray levels is:

$$
L_{\text{new}} = 2^4 = 16
$$

New image size:

$$
B_{\text{new}} = 1024 \times 1024 \times 4 = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bits} = 0.5 \text{ MB}
$$

**Observations:**
- File size reduced by **50%** (from 1 MB to 0.5 MB).
- The image will exhibit **false contouring** and **banding artifacts** because the human eye can distinguish more than 16 gray shades.
- Smooth gradients will appear as visible "steps" of constant gray.
- Loss of intensity fidelity, but storage economy is improved.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $M = 800$, $N = 600$, $C = 3$ channels, $k = 8$ bits/channel.

**(i) Total file size in KB (3 marks):**

$$
