## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q1. KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, PEAET745]**
> **Define the Sliding Window Approach in object detection. State its two main computational bottlenecks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Sliding Window Approach is a brute-force search technique in which a fixed-size rectangular window is moved across all spatial positions of an image and, optionally, across multiple scales via an image pyramid, evaluating a classifier or feature at each location.

Two main computational bottlenecks are:

1. **Combinatorial explosion of positions and scales:** The number of sub-windows grows as $\mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{H W S}{s_x s_y}\right)$, easily exceeding $10^8$ for typical webcam images.
2. **Classifier cost per window:** Even an efficient classifier, when evaluated millions of times, becomes the runtime bottleneck. This is precisely what Haar *Cascades* (early rejection) and HOG + Linear SVM (compact features) are designed to mitigate.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for position-scale cost, 1 mark for classifier cost.)*

---

> **[Q2. KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, PEAET745]**
> **What is the role of the Integral Image in the Viola-Jones face detector? Why is this representation preferred over direct pixel access?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Integral Image $I_{\text{int}}(x, y)$ stores, at each pixel, the cumulative sum of all pixel intensities in the rectangle from the origin $(0, 0)$ to that pixel. It is computed in a single pass over the image.

Its role is to allow any rectangular sum to be computed in $O(1)$ time using only four array lookups:

$$
S = I_{\text{int}}(x_2, y_2) - I_{\text{int}}(x_1 - 1, y_2) - I_{\text{int}}(x_2, y_1 - 1) + I_{\text{int}}(x_1 - 1, y_1 - 1)
$$

This is preferred over direct pixel access because a Haar-like feature evaluation would otherwise require summing all pixels in two large rectangles — an $O(hw)$ operation — which is prohibitively expensive when features are evaluated millions of times per frame. The integral image converts this to constant time, enabling real-time detection.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the four-lookup formula, 1 mark for the speed justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice: Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* *(chosen as Question A)*

**(a)** Explain the **Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)** descriptor in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the steps involved in computing the HOG feature vector for a given image patch. Mention the typical cell and block sizes used in pedestrian detection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $6 \times 6$ grayscale image patch is divided into $3 \times 3$ cells (each cell is $2 \times 2$ pixels). Compute the HOG feature descriptor using **2 bins** covering the orientation ranges $[0°, 180°)$ and $[180°, 360°)$. Assume gradients are pre-computed as follows:

$$
G = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\Theta = \begin{pmatrix} 30° & 200° \\ 150° & 10° \end{pmatrix}
$$

for each of the 4 cells. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HOG Descriptor — Theory (7 marks):**

HOG is a dense, local feature descriptor that captures the distribution of intensity gradient orientations in localized portions of an image. The steps are:

1. **Gradient Computation:** Apply 1-D centered kernels $[-1, 0, 1]$ (horizontal) and $[-1, 0, 1]^T$ (vertical) to compute $G_x$, $G_y$. Then derive magnitude $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ and orientation $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$.

2. **Cell Histograms:** Divide the image patch into small connected regions called *cells*. For each cell, build a 1-D histogram of gradient orientations, weighted by gradient magnitude. Dalal-Triggs uses **9 bins** for unsigned gradients ($0°$–$180°$) in $20°$ increments.

3. **Block Normalization:** Group cells into *blocks* (typically **$2 \times 2$ cells**, i.e., $16 \times 16$ pixels in pedestrian detection). Concatenate the cell histograms within the block and apply L2-Hys normalization to gain illumination invariance.

4. **Descriptor Concatenation:** Slide the block window across the image with overlap (typically 50%) and concatenate all normalized block vectors to form the final HOG feature vector.

5. **Classification:** The HOG descriptor is fed into a classifier (typically a linear SVM) to detect objects (e.g., pedestrians).

> **Typical sizes (Dalal-Triggs pedestrian detector):** cell = $8 \times 8$ pixels; block = $2 \times 2$ cells = $16 \times 16$ pixels; block stride = $8$ pixels (50% overlap); detection window = $64 \times 128$ pixels; bins = 9 (unsigned, $0°$–$180°$).

*(7 marks — 1 mark per step + 1 mark for typical sizes + 1 mark for block diagram if drawn.)*

**(b) HOG Computation (7 marks):**

Given a single cell:

$$
G = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\Theta = \begin{pmatrix} 30° & 200° \\ 150° & 10° \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Classify each pixel into a bin:**

| Pixel | Magnitude $G$ | Orientation $\theta$ | Bin |
|-------|--------------:|---------------------:|:---:|
| (0,0) | 2 | 30° | 1 ([0°, 180°)) |
| (0,1) | 4 | 200° | 2 ([180°, 360°)) |
| (1,0) | 3 | 150° | 1 ([0°, 180°)) |
| (1,1) | 5 | 10° | 1 ([0°, 180°)) |

**Step 2 — Sum magnitudes per bin:**

$$
h_1 = 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 \quad \text{(Bin 1)}
$$

$$
h_2 = 4 \quad \text{(Bin 2)}
$$

**Step 3 — HOG cell descriptor:**

$$
\mathbf{h}_{\text{cell}} = \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Block-level descriptor (assume $2 \times 2$ cells in a block).** Let the four cells' descriptors be:

$$
C_{00} = \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
C_{01} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
C_{10} = \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
C_{11} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Concatenate:

$$
\mathbf{v} = (10,\; 4,\; 8,\; 6,\; 12,\; 3,\; 7,\; 9)^T \in \mathbb{R}^{8}
$$

Compute L2 norm:

$$
\|v\|_2 = \sqrt{10^2 + 4^2 + 8^2 + 6^2 + 12^2 + 3^2 + 7^2 + 9^2} = \sqrt{100 + 16 + 64 + 36 + 144 + 9 + 49 + 81}
$$

$$
\|v\|_2 = \sqrt{499} \approx 22.338
$$

**Step 5 — L2 Normalize:**

$$
v_{\text{norm}} = \frac{1}{22.338}(10,\; 4,\; 8,\; 6,\; 12,\; 3,\; 7,\; 9)^T \approx (0.448,\; 0.179,\; 0.358,\; 0.269,\; 0.537,\; 0.134,\; 0.313,\; 0.403)^T
$$

**Step 6 — Concatenate over all blocks** to form the final HOG descriptor (e.g., $7 \times 15 = 105$ blocks for a $64 \times 128$ window, giving a $105 \times 4 \times 9 = 3780$-dim vector in the canonical pedestrian detector).

*(7 marks — 2 marks for bin classification, 2 marks for histogram sum, 1 mark for block concatenation, 1 mark for L2 norm, 1 mark for normalization.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* *(alternative choice)*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Viola-Jones face detection framework**. Discuss the role of Haar-like features, the Integral Image, AdaBoost, and the Cascade structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $4 \times 4$ Haar-like feature (two-rectangle horizontal) is to be evaluated on a $5 \times 5$ image using the Integral Image. The Integral Image is given as:

$$
I_{\text{int}} = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 1 & 3 & 6 & 10 \\
2 & 6 & 12 & 20 & 30 \\
6 & 14 & 26 & 42 & 62 \\
12 & 26 & 46 & 74 & 110 \\
20 & 42 & 74 & 120 & 180
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Compute the value of a horizontal two-rectangle Haar feature covering rows $[0, 1]$ and $[1, 2]$, columns $[1, 3]$ (i.e., top-left at $(1, 0)$, bottom-right at $(2, 3)$). **Use the standard four-lookup formula.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Viola-Jones Framework (7 marks):**

The Viola-Jones framework (2001) is a real-time object detection framework consisting of four key ideas:

1. **Haar-like Features:** Rectangular features that compute the difference between sums of pixel intensities in adjacent white and black regions. Three types exist: two-rectangle (edge), three-rectangle (line), and four-rectangle (diagonal). The total feature pool in a $24 \times 24$ window exceeds **160,000** features.

2. **Integral Image:** A precomputed 2-D array where each entry is the sum of all pixels in the rectangle from the origin. This makes any rectangular sum computable in $O(1)$ time.

3. **AdaBoost:** A meta-learner that selects a small subset (e.g., 200 out of 160,000) of highly discriminative Haar features and combines them with weighted voting to form a strong classifier $C(x) = \operatorname{sign}(\sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x))$.

4. **Cascade of Classifiers:** Strong classifiers are arranged in stages of increasing complexity. Each stage rejects a large fraction of non-object sub-windows, allowing deeper stages to focus only on hard, promising candidates. The final detection is the AND of all stage decisions.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Image → Integral Image → Haar Features → AdaBoost → Cascade Stage 1 → ... → Stage K → Detection
```

*(7 marks — 1.5 marks per component, 1 mark for block diagram.)*

**(b) Haar Feature Computation (7 marks):**

The feature is a horizontal two-rectangle feature, with:
- **Top rectangle (white):** rows 0–1, columns 1–3.
- **Bottom rectangle (black):** rows 1–2, columns 1–3.

In $(x, y)$ indexing (row $y$, column $x$), with the convention that the top-left corner is $(0, 0)$:

For the **white (top) rectangle** with corners:
- Top-left: $(1, 0)$, Bottom-right: $(3, 1)$ — i.e., columns 1, 2, 3 and rows 0, 1.
  - $A = I_{\text{int}}(3, 1) = 12$
  - $B = I_{\text{int}}(0, 1) = 1$
  - $C = I_{\text{int}}(3, -1) = 0$ (clamped)
  - $D = I_{\text{int}}(0, -1) = 0$ (clamped)
  - $S_{\text{white}} = 12 - 1 - 0 + 0 = 11$

For the **black (bottom) rectangle** with corners:
- Top-left: $(1, 1)$, Bottom-right: $(3, 2)$ — i.e., columns 1, 2, 3 and rows 1, 2.
  - $A = I_{\text{int}}(3, 2) = 26$
  - $B = I_{\text{int}}(0, 2) = 6$
  - $C = I_{\text{int}}(3, 0) = 6$
  - $D = I_{\text{int}}(0, 0) = 0$
  - $S_{\text{black}} = 26 - 6 - 6 + 0 = 14$

**Feature value:**

$$
f = S_{\text{white}} - S_{\text{black}} = 11 - 14 = -3
$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks for naming the four corners, 2 marks for the white sum, 2 marks for the black sum, 1 mark for the final subtraction.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing $(x, y)$ convention:** The Integral Image indexing must be clearly stated. Many students mix row and column, leading to incorrect corner labels. Always state your convention first.
> 2. **Off-by-one in four-lookup formula:** Forgetting the $\pm 1$ shifts in $I_{\text{int}}(x_1 - 1, y_2)$, etc. Re-derive the formula once and write it down at the start of your answer.
> 3. **HOG bin boundaries:** Students often forget whether gradients are signed (0°–360°, 18 bins @ 20°) or unsigned (0°–180°, 9 bins @ 20°). Dalal-Triggs default is **unsigned**.
> 4. **Confusing SSD and NCC:** SSD is a *dissimilarity* measure (lower is better); NCC is a *similarity* measure (higher is better). Template matching argmin vs argmax matters.
> 5. **Cascade stages:** Many students draw the cascade as parallel — it is strictly **sequential**. A sub-window must pass Stage 1 to enter Stage 2.
> 6. **No mention of multi-scale:** Sliding window without an image pyramid is *not* scale-invariant. Always mention the image pyramid in any sliding-window question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Carry):**

- [ ] **Sliding Window** = exhaustive sweep over $(x, y, s)$ with a classifier. **Cost is $O(H W S / (s_x s_y))$.**
- [ ] **Image pyramid** converts multi-scale search into fixed-window search. **Scale factor $k \in (0.6, 0.9)$** typical.
- [ ] **NMS** (Non-Maximum Suppression) collapses overlapping high-scoring windows into one. **Use IoU threshold $\approx 0.5$.**
- [ ] **Haar feature** = difference of sums over adjacent rectangles. **Three types:** edge (2-rect), line (3-rect), diagonal (4-rect).
- [ ] **Integral Image formula:** $I_{\text{int}}(x, y) = \sum_{x' \le x,\, y' \le y} I(x', y')$. **Any rectangle sum in $O(1)$ via $S = A - B - C + D$.**
- [ ] **AdaBoost** picks $T \ll 160{,}000$ best features and combines them with weights $\alpha_t$.
- [ ] **Cascade** = sequential AND of stage decisions. **Each stage tuned to detection rate $\ge 0.999$ and false-positive rate $\le 0.5$.**
- [ ] **HOG gradients:** $G_x = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$; same for $G_y$. Use centered difference.
- [ ] **HOG magnitude:** $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$. **Orientation:** $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$.
- [ ] **HOG parameters (Dalal-Triggs):** cell = $8 \times 8$, block = $2 \times 2$ cells, **9 unsigned bins**, 50% block overlap.
- [ ] **L2-Hys normalization:** clip to 0.2, then re-normalize. **Gives invariance to linear illumination change.**
- [ ] **Template Matching metrics:** SSD (lower better), NCC ($\in [-1, 1]$, 1 = perfect), CCoeff (higher better).
- [ ] **NCC is invariant to linear intensity changes** of the patch (proved in §3.3). SSD is *not* invariant.
- [ ] **Template matching is not scale/rotation invariant** — requires multi-scale and multi-rotation search.
- [ ] **Industrial use cases:** ADAS (HOG), Face autofocus (Haar), PCB inspection (Template), Security CCTV (Cascade).
- [ ] **One-line summary of the module:** *Sliding windows locate, Haar/HOG/Template describe, cascades reject, NMS refines.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Analysis and 3D Vision – Background Subtraction, Frame Differencing, Temporal Differencing, and Basic Optical Flow Concepts (Lucas–Kanade Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motion Analysis and 3D Vision – Background Subtraction, Frame Differencing, Temporal Differencing, and Basic Optical Flow Concepts (Lucas–Kanade Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Motion Analysis in Computer Vision?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Motion Analysis* is the branch of computer vision concerned with the **detection, estimation, and tracking of object movement** across a sequence of image frames. It deals with the recovery of **2D motion fields (image velocity)** and the inference of **3D structure from motion cues**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine watching a highway through a fixed CCTV camera. Cars zip forward, the road stays still, and trees sway slowly. Your brain, without thinking, instantly classifies pixels as "moving vehicle" or "stationary road." This is exactly what *Motion Analysis* teaches a machine to do — to *digitally perceive* which parts of a frame are in motion and *quantify* that motion. The four foundational techniques every KTU student must master are **Background Subtraction**, **Frame Differencing**, **Temporal Differencing**, and **Optical Flow (Lucas–Kanade)**.

---

### 1.2 Background Subtraction (BS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Background Subtraction is a pixel-level technique that builds a statistical model of the *static scene (background)* and identifies *foreground objects* by thresholding the difference between the current frame and the background model.

**Analogy — "The Empty Room Test":**
Think of photographing your room when nobody is in it. That picture becomes your "background." Tomorrow, when you photograph the room again with a friend standing in it, you simply **subtract the empty-room picture from the current picture** — what's left over (non-zero pixels) is your friend! In surveillance systems, this is precisely how a static CCTV camera detects intruders.

**Key Idea:**
$$B_t(x,y) = \alpha \cdot F_t(x,y) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot B_{t-1}(x,y)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate (0 < α < 1)**, $F_t$ is the current frame, and $B_t$ is the updated background.

---

### 1.3 Frame Differencing

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Frame Differencing (also called *Image Differencing*) detects motion by computing the **absolute pixel-wise difference between two consecutive frames** and thresholding it.

**Analogy — "The Stop-Motion Trick":**
Remember flipping through two photographs of a child — one with hands up, one with hands down. The places where the child's silhouette *changed* between the two photos mark the motion. Frame differencing performs exactly that subtraction, frame-by-frame, in real time.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$D_t(x,y) = \vert F_t(x,y) - F_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$$

---

### 1.4 Temporal Differencing

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Temporal Differencing is a special case of frame differencing in which the previous frame's pixels are used as the *background reference*. It is a **lightweight, self-adaptive** background model that doesn't require an explicit training phase.

**Analogy — "Following the Last Known Shadow":**
Instead of remembering what the room looked like a week ago, you only remember what it looked like **a second ago**. If something has now changed, you flag it. The advantage? The model *automatically adapts* to gradual changes (like lighting shifts at sunset).

---

### 1.5 Optical Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Optical Flow* is the **2D vector field of apparent motion of brightness patterns** between two frames. It estimates, for every pixel $(x, y)$, the velocity vector $(u, v)$ such that the pixel moves from $(x, y)$ at time $t$ to $(x+u, y+v)$ at time $t+dt$.

**Analogy — "The River of Pixels":**
Imagine a flowing river. Each leaf on the surface has a velocity — up, down, sideways. Optical flow tries to compute the *velocity of every pixel* as if the image were a flowing liquid. The **Lucas–Kanade method** is one of the most famous algorithms to compute this flow.

---

### 1.6 Lucas–Kanade Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The Lucas–Kanade (LK) method is a **differential, sparse optical flow technique** that estimates motion vectors under two key assumptions: **(1) Brightness Constancy** and **(2) Spatial Coherence (constant flow in a small neighborhood).**

**Analogy — "A Neighborhood Vote":**
If you're trying to figure out which way a small patch of image is moving, instead of looking at a single pixel (which gives an *under-determined* problem), the LK method looks at a **5×5 (or larger) window around it** and uses *all* the pixels there to "vote" for the most likely motion vector via **least-squares estimation**.

---

### 1.7 The Three Foundational Optical Flow Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Brightness Constancy Assumption:** Pixel intensities remain unchanged as they move: $I(x, y, t) = I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt)$.
> 2. **Spatial Coherence / Small Motion Assumption:** Neighboring pixels belong to the same surface and have **similar motion**.
> 3. **Temporal Persistence (small $dt$):** Motion between consecutive frames is small enough for a **first-order Taylor approximation** to be valid.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Background Subtraction — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Pipeline:**
1. **Initialization:** Capture the first $N$ frames (typically $N=20$–$50$) with an empty scene to compute the *mean* $\mu(x,y)$ and *variance* $\sigma^2(x,y)$ of each pixel — this is the **Gaussian background model**.
2. **Foreground Detection:** For the incoming frame $F_t$, compute $|F_t(x,y) - \mu(x,y)| > k\sigma(x,y)$. Pixels exceeding threshold $k$ (typically $k=2.5$ to $3$) are marked as **foreground**.
3. **Background Update:** Recursively update $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ using a learning rate $\alpha$.
4. **Post-Processing:** Apply **morphological opening/closing** to remove noise blobs.

**Variants (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Running Gaussian Average** (Wren et al.)
- **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** — Stauffer & Grimson
- **Running Median Filter**
- **Codebook Model** — Kim et al.

**Limitations:** Fails under **dynamic backgrounds** (fountains, waving trees), **sudden illumination changes**, **camera jitter**, and **bootstrapping problems** (no empty scene available).

---

### 2.2 Frame Differencing — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Pipeline:**
1. Capture frame $F_{t-1}$ and store it.
2. Capture current frame $F_t$.
3. Compute absolute difference: $D_t(x,y) = |F_t(x,y) - F_{t-1}(x,y)|$.
4. Apply threshold $T$: if $D_t(x,y) > T$, the pixel is **moving**.
5. Apply connected component analysis to extract motion blobs.

**Advantage over Background Subtraction:** No background model training required — fully **adaptive**.
**Disadvantage:** Detects only the **edges/leading edges** of moving objects (the "**hollow object**" or "**double-edge**" problem), and is highly sensitive to the threshold $T$.

---

### 2.3 Temporal Differencing — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Pipeline:**
1. Maintain a *running estimate* of the background $B_{t-1}$ (often initialized as the first frame).
2. Compute $D_t = |F_t - B_{t-1}|$.
3. Threshold to obtain foreground mask.
4. Update $B_t = F_t$ (or use an exponential weighted average: $B_t = \alpha F_t + (1-\alpha)B_{t-1}$).

**Note:** Temporal differencing is essentially **frame differencing with a slow-adapting background**. The two terms are often used interchangeably in KTU textbooks; the distinction lies in *whether an explicit background model is maintained*.

---

### 2.4 Optical Flow — The Brightness Constancy Equation

Let $I(x, y, t)$ be the image intensity at pixel $(x, y)$ and time $t$. Under the **brightness constancy assumption**:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$$

Applying the **first-order Taylor series expansion** on the right-hand side:

$$I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt) \approx I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}dt$$

Subtracting $I(x,y,t)$ from both sides and dividing by $dt$:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** — the single most important equation in Module 4.

---

### 2.5 The Aperture Problem

The OFCE has **one equation and two unknowns** $(u, v)$. From a single pixel, the velocity is *under-determined* — we can only determine the **normal flow** (motion component along the intensity gradient), not the full velocity. This is the **aperture problem**.

**Solution (Lucas–Kanade):** Combine OFCE over a small spatial neighborhood of $n$ pixels and solve via **least squares**.

---

### 2.6 Lucas–Kanade Method — Operational Logic

1. Compute image gradients $I_x, I_y$ and temporal gradient $I_t$ at every pixel (using **Sobel** or **derivative-of-Gaussian** filters).
2. For each pixel $p$, take a window $W$ (e.g., 5×5) of $n$ surrounding pixels.
3. Stack the OFCE for all $n$ pixels into a matrix equation:

$$A \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{b}, \quad \text{where} \quad A = \begin{bmatrix} I_x(p_1) & I_y(p_1) \\ I_x(p_2) & I_y(p_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(p_n) & I_y(p_n) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{d} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -I_t(p_1) \\ -I_t(p_2) \\ \vdots \\ -I_t(p_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

4. Solve the **normal equations**: $A^T A \mathbf{d} = A^T \mathbf{b}$.
5. Provided $A^T A$ is **invertible** (i.e., the pixel is in a *textured* region, not an edge — related to the **Harris corner detector** condition), the flow is:

$$\mathbf{d} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Brightness Constancy | $I(x,y,t) = I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt)$ | Intensity invariant under motion |
| 2 | Optical Flow Constraint Eqn (OFCE) | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | Scalar constraint — 1 eqn, 2 unknowns |
| 3 | LK Least-Squares Solution | $\mathbf{d} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ | Velocity vector at pixel $p$ |
| 4 | Structure Tensor of LK | $A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | $2 \times 2$ matrix; must be invertible |
| 5 | Closed-form LK | $u = \frac{-I_t (I_y^2) + I_t (I_x I_y)}{D}$, $\;v = \frac{-I_t (I_x^2) + I_t (I_x I_y)}{D}$ | Where $D = (I_x^2)(I_y^2) - (I_x I_y)^2$ |
| 6 | Background Update (Running Avg.) | $B_t = \alpha F_t + (1-\alpha) B_{t-1}$ | $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ (learning rate) |
| 7 | Frame Difference | $D_t(x,y) = \vert F_t(x,y) - F_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$ | Motion mask before thresholding |
| 8 | Gaussian BG Foreground Rule | $\vert F_t(x,y) - \mu(x,y) \vert > k \sigma(x,y)$ | $k$ typically 2.5 – 3.0 |
| 9 | Harris-style Invertibility | $\det(A^T A) - \lambda \, \text{trace}(A^T A) > 0$ | Both eigenvalues large → corner |
| 10 | Pyramid Level Scaling (Coarse-to-Fine) | $u^{(L)} = 2 \cdot u^{(L+1)}$ | Used to handle *large* motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility:** Background subtraction powers CCTV surveillance, traffic monitoring, and gesture recognition. Lucas–Kanade optical flow underpins **SIFT feature tracking**, **video stabilization (e.g., YouTube's Hyperlapse)**, **autonomous driving (lane/pedestrian motion estimation)**, **action recognition in sports analytics**, and **medical imaging (heart motion in echocardiography)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)

**Starting Premise — Brightness Constancy Assumption:**

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt) \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **first-order Taylor series expansion** to the right-hand side of Eq. 1 around the point $(x, y, t)$. The general multivariable Taylor expansion of $f$ at $(x_0, y_0, t_0)$ is:

$$f(x_0 + \Delta x) \approx f(x_0) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial f}{\partial t}\Delta t + \text{higher order terms}$$

**Step 2:** Expanding $I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt)$ around $(x, y, t)$ and ignoring higher-order infinitesimals:

$$I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt) \approx I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}dt \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Eq. 2 into Eq. 1:

$$I(x,y,t) = I(x,y,t) + I_x \, dx + I_y \, dy + I_t \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $I(x,y,t)$ from both sides:

$$0 = I_x \, dx + I_y \, dy + I_t \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Divide throughout by $dt$ (assuming $dt \neq 0$), and use the kinematic definition of velocity components:

$$u = \frac{dx}{dt}, \quad v = \frac{dy}{dt}$$

**Step 6:** The Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE) emerges:

$$\boxed{I_x \, u + I_y \, v + I_t = 0}$$

This is a **scalar** equation in **two unknowns** $(u, v)$ — hence the *aperture problem*. The vector $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ is the **spatial intensity gradient**, and the motion component along this gradient direction is the only one recoverable from a single pixel.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lucas–Kanade Least-Squares Solution

**Step 1:** Suppose we have $n$ pixels $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$ inside a small window $W$ centered at $p$. We assume **constant flow** $(u, v)$ throughout $W$ (spatial coherence). The OFCE for pixel $p_j$ is:

$$I_x(p_j) u + I_y(p_j) v = -I_t(p_j), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Step 2:** Stack the $n$ equations into a matrix system $A \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_x(p_1) & I_y(p_1) \\ I_x(p_2) & I_y(p_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(p_n) & I_y(p_n) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -I_t(p_1) \\ -I_t(p_2) \\ \vdots \\ -I_t(p_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Since $A$ is a $(n \times 2)$ matrix with $n \gg 2$, the system is **over-determined**. The best-fit solution in the **least-squares sense** is obtained by minimizing the squared residual $J(u, v) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( I_x(p_j) u + I_y(p_j) v + I_t(p_j) \right)^2$.

**Step 4:** Set the partial derivatives $\partial J / \partial u = 0$ and $\partial J / \partial v = 0$, which yields the **normal equations**:

$$A^T A \mathbf{d} = A^T \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 5:** Expand $A^T A$ (the **structure tensor** or **second-moment matrix**):

$$A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} \sum_j I_x(p_j)^2 & \sum_j I_x(p_j) I_y(p_j) \\ \sum_j I_x(p_j) I_y(p_j) & \sum_j I_y(p_j)^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Provided $\det(A^T A) = \sum I_x^2 \cdot \sum I_y^2 - \left(\sum I_x I_y\right)^2 \neq 0$, the matrix is invertible. The closed-form flow is:

$$\mathbf{d} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 7:** Using Cramer's rule on the $2 \times 2$ system, the components of $\mathbf{d}$ are:

$$u = \frac{\sum I_y^2 \cdot \sum (-I_t I_x) - \sum I_x I_y \cdot \sum (-I_t I_y)}{\left(\sum I_x^2\right)\left(\sum I_y^2\right) - \left(\sum I_x I_y\right)^2}$$

$$v = \frac{\sum I_x^2 \cdot \sum (-I_t I_y) - \sum I_x I_y \cdot \sum (-I_t I_x)}{\left(\sum I_x^2\right)\left(\sum I_y^2\right) - \left(\sum I_x I_y\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The matrix $A^T A$ has **two eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$. If *both* are large → the pixel is a **corner** (well-textured), and LK works reliably. If *one* is large and the other is small → the pixel is on an **edge** (aperture problem persists). If *both* are small → the pixel is in a **flat region** (no information). This is mathematically equivalent to the **Harris corner detector** condition.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Method | Background Model Required? | Speed | Handles Illumination Change? | Handles Camera Motion? | Detects Object Interior? | Typical Threshold |
|--------|---------------------------|-------|------------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| Background Subtraction | Yes (Gaussian, GMM) | Medium | Poor (without color models) | Poor | Yes | $k = 2.5$–$3.0$ |
| Frame Differencing | No | Fast | Moderate | Moderate | No (only edges) | $T = 20$–$50$ |
| Temporal Differencing | Implicit (running avg.) | Fast | Good | Moderate | Partial | $T$ adaptive |
| Lucas–Kanade Optical Flow | No | Medium | Poor (violates brightness constancy) | Good (with pyramids) | Yes (dense per-pixel) | N/A (uses gradients) |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Lucas–Kanade on a 2×2 Patch)

Suppose a 2×2 window yields the following measured gradients (in pixel units):

$$I_x = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad I_y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad I_t = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -2 \\ -4 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the structure tensor:

$$A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} 2^2+1^2+1^2+2^2 & 2\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 1 \\ \cdot & 1^2+2^2+2^2+1^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determinant: $\det(A^T A) = 100 - 64 = 36 > 0$ ✓ (invertible — the window is well-textured).

Compute $A^T \mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{b} = -I_t = [3, 2, 4, 3]^T$:

$$A^T \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 2\cdot 3 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 4 + 2\cdot 3 \\ 1\cdot 3 + 2\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 18 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solve:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & -8 \\ -8 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 18 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} 36 \\ 36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Result:** The window has a uniform motion of **$(u, v) = (1, 1)$ pixel/frame** — i.e., moving diagonally down-right.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Background Subtraction Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Video Input Sequence] --> B[Frame F at time t]
    C[Background Model B at time t-1] --> D[Pixel-wise Subtraction]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Absolute Difference]
    E --> F[Threshold T]
    F --> G{Exceeds T?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Foreground Mask FG]
    G -- No --> I[Background Region]
    H --> J[Morphological Cleaning]
    I --> K[Update BG Model]
    J --> K
    K --> C
    H --> L[Connected Components / Blob Detection]
    L --> M[Detected Moving Objects]
```

**Block-Level Explanation:**
- The **video stream** provides a stream of frames; the **background model** is the *learned reference* of the static scene.
- The **subtraction + threshold** stage is the heart of BS — any pixel that deviates from the model by more than $T$ is flagged as foreground.
- The **background update stage** is *adaptive*: the model is *re-trained* on the current frame with weight $\alpha$ to handle gradual changes (e.g., a parked car that becomes background).
- The **morphological cleaning** removes salt-and-pepper noise, and the **connected component analysis** groups adjacent foreground pixels into discrete moving objects.

---

### 4.2 Lucas–Kanade Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Frame at time t: I x,y,t] --> P2[Compute Spatial Gradients Ix, Iy]
    P3[Frame at time t+1: I x+dx,y+dy,t+dt] --> P4[Compute Temporal Gradient It]
    P2 --> P5[Form Matrix A from window W]
    P4 --> P6[Form Vector b from window W]
    P5 --> P7[Compute Structure Tensor A transposed A]
    P7 --> P8{Invertible? det A transposed A greater than 0}
    P8 -- No --> P9[Reject pixel: flat or edge region]
    P8 -- Yes --> P10[Compute d = A transposed A inverse A transposed b]
    P10 --> P11[Output Optical Flow Vector u, v]
    P6 --> P10
```

**Block-Level Explanation:**
- Inputs are **two consecutive frames** — the algorithm needs at least two time steps to compute $I_t$.
- The **gradient computation stage** uses Sobel filters or Gaussian derivatives — this is the *image preprocessing* layer.
- The **structure tensor** $A^T A$ encapsulates the local second-moment structure of the image; its invertibility decides *whether* LK can produce a reliable flow at this pixel.
- The **least-squares solver** is the *core* of LK — it solves the *over-determined* system of OFCEs for $(u, v)$.
- Output is a **dense or sparse flow field**, depending on whether LK is applied at every pixel or only at feature points (e.g., **Shi–Tomasi good-features-to-track** corners).

---

### 4.3 Coarse-to-Fine Lucas–Kanade (Pyramid Scheme)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Level L: Coarsest image] --> L1[Compute LK flow at L]
    L1 --> L2[Upsample flow to level L-1]
    L2 --> L3[Use as initial guess]
    L3 --> L4[Compute LK flow at L-1]
    L4 --> L5{In finest level?}
    L5 -- No --> L2
    L5 -- Yes --> L6[Final dense flow at full resolution]
```

**Block-Level Explanation:**
- The **Gaussian image pyramid** is built by progressively downsampling the input image.
- LK is first applied at the **coarsest level** (smallest image), where motion is small relative to image size — this is valid under the small-motion assumption.
- The estimated flow is then **upsampled and used as an initialization** at the next finer level, where LK solves only the *residual* flow.
- This **iterative refinement** allows LK to handle *large motions* (e.g., fast cars) that would otherwise violate the small-$dt$ Taylor expansion.

---

### 4.4 Comparison of Motion Analysis Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MA[Motion Analysis Methods] --> BS[Background Subtraction]
    MA --> FD[Frame Differencing]
    MA --> TD[Temporal Differencing]
    MA --> OF[Optical Flow]
    BS --> BS1[Gaussian / GMM / Median]
    FD --> FD1[Consecutive Frame Subtraction]
    TD --> TD1[Adaptive Background Comparison]
    OF --> OF1[Lucas-Kanade / Horn-Schunck / Farneback]
    OF1 --> OF2[Sparse via Feature Points]
    OF1 --> OF3[Dense Per-Pixel]
    OF1 --> OF4[Pyramid-based Large Motion]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the **aperture problem** in optical flow estimation. How does the **Lucas–Kanade method** overcome it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The aperture problem refers to the inability to determine the **true 2D motion** of a pixel from a local observation, because the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ provides only one equation in two unknowns $(u, v)$. Through a small aperture, only the motion component *normal* to the intensity gradient (called the **normal flow**) can be measured; the *tangential* component is unobservable.
The **Lucas–Kanade method** overcomes this by assuming **spatial coherence** — that all pixels in a small neighborhood share the same flow $(u, v)$. By stacking the OFCEs for $n$ neighboring pixels, an **over-determined system** is formed and solved using **least squares**, yielding a unique flow vector $\mathbf{d} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$, provided the structure tensor $A^T A$ is invertible (i.e., the neighborhood is a textured corner region). **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for OFCE reasoning, 1 for LK solution)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare **Background Subtraction** and **Frame Differencing** as motion detection techniques. List **two advantages** of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Background Subtraction | Frame Differencing |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Background Model | Yes (explicit) | No (uses previous frame directly) |
| Detects Object Interior | Yes (full object mask) | No (only edges / "hollow" objects) |
| Computational Cost | Higher (model update) | Lower (one subtraction) |
| Robustness to Slow Motion | Good | Poor (objects may "fade out") |

**Two advantages of Background Subtraction:** (1) Detects *complete* foreground masks, not just leading edges; (2) robust to *temporally sparse* motion.
**Two advantages of Frame Differencing:** (1) Self-adaptive with no training phase; (2) computationally cheap and easily real-time. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** from the **brightness constancy assumption** using a first-order Taylor series expansion. Clearly state the **assumptions** involved. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Lucas–Kanade method** in detail. Explain how it solves the **aperture problem** using a local neighborhood and **least-squares estimation**. State the role of the **structure tensor** $A^T A$ and the condition for its **invertibility**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **State the Brightness Constancy Assumption** (1 mark):
The intensity of a moving point remains constant over short time intervals:
$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$$

2. **Apply first-order Taylor series** to the right-hand side (2 marks):
$$I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt) \approx I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}dt$$

3. **Derive the OFCE** (2 marks):
$$0 = I_x \, dx + I_y \, dy + I_t \, dt \;\Rightarrow\; I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0, \;\text{where}\; u = dx/dt,\; v = dy/dt$$

4. **State additional assumptions** (2 marks):
   - *Small motion:* displacement $(dx, dy)$ must be less than 1 pixel for the Taylor truncation to be valid.
   - *Smooth image intensity field:* the derivatives $I_x, I_y, I_t$ must exist and be well-defined (requires pre-smoothing with a Gaussian).
   - *Spatial coherence:* neighboring pixels share similar motion (used in part b).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Problem identification** (1 mark): The OFCE has two unknowns $(u, v)$ in one equation — the **aperture problem**.

2. **Spatial Coherence Assumption** (1 mark): Within a small window $W$ of $n$ pixels, the flow $(u, v)$ is **constant**. The OFCE is applied at each pixel $p_j \in W$:

$$I_x(p_j) u + I_y(p_j) v = -I_t(p_j)$$

3. **Matrix formulation** (1 mark): The $n$ equations form an over-determined system $A \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{b}$, with $A$ being $(n \times 2)$.

4. **Least-squares solution** (2 marks): The optimal $(u, v)$ minimizes $\sum_j (A_j \mathbf{d} + \mathbf{b}_j)^2$, leading to the normal equations $A^T A \mathbf{d} = A^T \mathbf{b}$, and the solution:

$$\mathbf{d} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

5. **Structure tensor and invertibility** (2 marks): The matrix
$$A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$
must be invertible, which requires $\det(A^T A) = \left(\sum I_x^2\right)\left(\sum I_y^2\right) - \left(\sum I_x I_y\right)^2 \neq 0$. Geometrically, *both eigenvalues* of $A^T A$ must be large — the window must contain **corner-like texture**, not edges or flat regions. This is the **Harris corner condition** and links LK directly to **good-features-to-track** selection.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **background subtraction algorithm** in detail. Discuss its **pipeline**, the role of the **Gaussian background model**, and **two major limitations** of the method. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With neat diagrams and equations, explain the **Temporal Differencing** and **Frame Differencing** methods for motion detection. Compare their **performance** under **slow motion**, **fast motion**, and **dynamic backgrounds**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Algorithm Overview** (1 mark): Background Subtraction maintains a *statistical model* of the static scene and compares each incoming frame against it to detect **foreground** regions.

2. **Gaussian Background Model** (2 marks): Each pixel $(x, y)$ is modeled as an independent Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu(x,y), \sigma^2(x,y))$. The model is initialized by computing mean and variance over the first $N$ empty frames. A pixel is foreground if:

$$|F_t(x, y) - \mu(x, y)| > k \, \sigma(x, y), \quad k = 2.5\text{ to }3.0$$

3. **Adaptive Update** (2 marks): With learning rate $\alpha \in (0, 1]$, the model is updated as:
$$\mu_t = \alpha F_t + (1-\alpha)\mu_{t-1}, \quad \sigma_t^2 = \alpha (F_t - \mu_t)^2 + (1-\alpha)\sigma_{t-1}^2$$

4. **Two Major Limitations** (2 marks):
   - **Dynamic backgrounds:** Waving trees, fountains, or shadows cause spurious "foreground" detections.
   - **Bootstrap problem:** The model requires an *empty* scene for training — not always available in crowded scenarios.
   - (Also acceptable: *sudden illumination changes*, *camera jitter*.)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Frame Differencing** (2 marks): $D_t(x,y) = |F_t(x,y) - F_{t-1}(x,y)|$, thresholded by $T$. **Advantages:** simple, fast, no training. **Disadvantages:** detects only *leading and trailing edges* of moving objects, not the interior (the "**hollow object**" problem); fails under slow motion because consecutive frames are nearly identical.

2. **Temporal Differencing** (2 marks): A running estimate of the background $B_t = \alpha F_t + (1-\alpha)B_{t-1}$ is maintained and compared with the current frame. $D_t = |F_t - B_{t-1}|$. It is essentially frame differencing with **slowly adapting** background.

3. **Comparative Performance** (3 marks):
   - **Slow Motion:** Frame differencing fails (no significant change); temporal differencing with low $\alpha$ works.
   - **Fast Motion:** Frame differencing captures large motion blobs; temporal differencing may misclassify slow background as foreground.
   - **Dynamic Backgrounds:** Both struggle, but temporal differencing's adaptive nature provides *some* resilience if $\alpha$ is set correctly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the brightness constancy assumption** before deriving OFCE: This is a 1-mark loss in nearly every 7-mark question on optical flow.
> 2. **Confusing $A^T A$ with $A A^T$:** The structure tensor is *always* $A^T A$ (a $2 \times 2$ matrix), not $A A^T$ (an $n \times n$ matrix). Examiners specifically look for $A^T A$.
> 3. **Omitting the invertibility condition:** Stating $\mathbf{d} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ *without* mentioning the determinant condition loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up "Frame Differencing" and "Temporal Differencing":** They are *not* the same — frame differencing uses the previous frame, temporal differencing uses an *exponentially weighted* running average.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption of small motion:** The Taylor expansion is *first-order*; explicitly stating $dt \rightarrow 0$ or "small displacement" is mandatory.
> 6. **Not connecting LK to Harris corners:** Examiners love asking this link — if $A^T A$ has two large eigenvalues, the pixel is a *corner* and LK works; if not, it fails. Always mention this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Motion Analysis**

- **Motion Analysis** = detection + estimation + tracking of object movement in image sequences.
- **Background Subtraction (BS):** Pixel-level difference from a *learned* background model (Gaussian / GMM / Median). Best for **static cameras**.
- **Frame Differencing:** $|F_t - F_{t-1}| > T$. Simple, fast, but produces *hollow* object masks.
- **Temporal Differencing:** $|F_t - B_{t-1}| > T$, where $B_{t-1}$ is a *running weighted average* of past frames. Adaptive, no training phase.
- **Optical Flow:** 2D velocity field $(u, v)$ of apparent pixel motion.
- **Brightness Constancy Assumption:** $I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$.
- **OFCE (Optical Flow Constraint Equation):** $\boxed{I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0}$ — **1 equation, 2 unknowns** → *aperture problem*.
- **Lucas–Kanade Method:** Solves OFCE over a *neighborhood* $W$ using **least squares** under the **spatial coherence** assumption.
- **Lucas–Kanade Solution:** $\mathbf{d} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$, where $A$ stacks spatial gradients and $\mathbf{b} = -I_t$.
- **Structure Tensor $A^T A$:** $2 \times 2$ second-moment matrix; invertibility requires *both* eigenvalues large → the window is a **Harris corner**.
- **Coarse-to-Fine (Pyramid) LK:** Used to handle **large motions** that violate the small-$dt$ Taylor assumption.
- **Link to Harris Corner Detector:** The LK condition $\det(A^T A) - \lambda \cdot \text{trace}(A^T A) > 0$ is *mathematically equivalent* to the **Harris** response.
- **Sparse vs Dense Optical Flow:** LK applied at *feature points* (Shi–Tomasi) = sparse (e.g., SIFT tracking); Farneback / TV-L1 = dense.
- **Limitations of LK:** Fails on uniform regions, fails on edges (aperture persists), fails under large motion (use pyramids), fails under illumination change (violates brightness constancy).
- **Real-world Applications:** Surveillance CCTV, video stabilization (YouTube Hyperlapse), autonomous vehicles (lane and pedestrian motion), sports analytics, medical imaging (cardiac motion), augmented reality (SLAM front-end), gesture recognition.
- **Common KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Part (a) = Derive OFCE / explain assumptions (7 marks); Part (b) = Describe Lucas–Kanade / structure tensor / Harris link (7 marks).
- **Common KTU 3-Mark Question Pattern:** Define aperture problem, compare BS vs FD, state Lucas–Kanade assumptions, or define optical flow.
- **Thresholding tip:** For BS, use $k\sigma$ rule; for FD, $T = 20$–$50$ (grayscale); always post-process with morphological opening then closing.
- **Final exam mantra:** *"Brightness constancy gives one equation; Lucas–Kanade uses a neighborhood to vote for the missing second equation."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracking Algorithms (Kalman Filter, Mean Shift)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tracking Algorithms (Kalman Filter, Mean Shift)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Tracking in Computer Vision — Formal Definition

**Object Tracking** is the process of locating a moving object (or multiple objects) over time in a sequence of frames captured by a camera. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, tracking algorithms are responsible for estimating the state (position, velocity, scale, orientation) of a target across consecutive video frames, even in the presence of noise, occlusion, and illumination changes.

The KTU 2024 Computer Vision syllabus specifically prescribes two canonical tracking paradigms:

- **Kalman Filter (KF)** — A *model-based, parametric* recursive Bayesian estimator.
- **Mean Shift Algorithm** — A *non-parametric, density-gradient* based mode-seeking technique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Tracking algorithms use prior information about the object's state and appearance to predict and update its location in subsequent frames. The Kalman Filter assumes a linear Gaussian dynamic system, while Mean Shift iteratively climbs the gradient of a probability density function defined over a target region.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

#### Kalman Filter — The "Weather Predictor" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Predicting a Train's Position:**
> Imagine you are a station master trying to estimate where train number **KTU-Express** will be exactly 10 seconds from now. You have two sources of information:
> 1. **Your Prediction (Physics Model)**: "The train moves at 80 km/h, so in 10 seconds it should be ~222 meters ahead." (This is the **Predict** step).
> 2. **Your Measurement (Noisy GPS)**: A GPS sensor on the train says it is 200 meters ahead, but GPS is *noisy* and *imprecise*. (This is the **Update** step).
> 
> You never fully trust either source. Instead, you take a **weighted average** — the **Kalman Gain** $K_k$ decides how much to trust the measurement vs. the prediction. This weighted estimate becomes the new "best guess" of the train's true position.

#### Mean Shift — The "Hill Climber on a Terrain" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Finding the Densest Crowd in a Stadium:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into a stadium where the density of people represents the *probability* of finding the object. You want to find the *peak density region* (the densest crowd — the object).
> 
> 1. At any point, you can feel how many people are around you in a small circle (the **kernel window**).
> 2. You shift your position toward the **mean (centroid)** of the people in your window.
> 3. You repeat this until the window stops moving — you've found the mode (peak).
> 
> This iterative "shifting toward the mean" is the **Mean Shift** algorithm.

---

### 1.3 Why Tracking Algorithms Are Essential in Engineering

| Application Domain | Role of Tracking |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Track pedestrians, lanes, other cars across frames |
| **Medical Imaging** | Track tumor growth or surgical instruments in MRI/US |
| **Surveillance Systems** | Follow suspects across CCTV networks |
| **Robotics** | Visual servoing, hand-eye coordination |
| **AR/VR** | Track hand gestures, head pose, fiducial markers |
| **Sports Analytics** | Track player movement for performance metrics |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **State Vector Dimension ($n$)**: Number of variables being tracked (e.g., $n=4$ for $[x, y, v_x, v_y]^T$).
- **Measurement Vector Dimension ($m$)**: Number of measured variables (e.g., $m=2$ for $[x, y]^T$).
- **Process Noise Covariance ($Q$)**: Uncertainty in the dynamic model. Units: **state-units²**.
- **Measurement Noise Covariance ($R$)**: Uncertainty in sensor readings. Units: **measurement-units²**.
- **Frames Per Second (FPS)**: Real-time tracking typically requires $\geq 15$ FPS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kalman Filter — Theoretical Foundation

The Kalman Filter is a **two-stage recursive Bayesian estimator** operating on the assumption that the system is **linear** and all noise is **Gaussian with zero mean**.

#### 2.1.1 The Two Fundamental Equations (System Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Linear Dynamic System is described by TWO equations:**

**State Transition Equation (Process Model):**
$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_{k-1}$$

**Measurement Equation (Observation Model):**
$$z_k = H x_k + v_k$$

Where:
- $x_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ = **State vector** at time $k$ (e.g., position, velocity)
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ = **State transition matrix** (relates state at $k-1$ to $k$)
- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ = **Control input matrix**
- $u_k \in \mathbb{R}^p$ = **Control input vector**
- $H \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ = **Observation matrix** (maps state to measurement space)
- $w_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ = **Process noise** with covariance $Q$
- $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ = **Measurement noise** with covariance $R$
- $z_k \in \mathbb{R}^m$ = **Measurement vector** at time $k$

---

#### 2.1.2 The Five Kalman Filter Equations (TWO Predict + THREE Update)

**PREDICT STAGE (Time Update):**

**Equation 1 — A Priori State Estimate:**
$$\hat{x}_{k|k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1|k-1} + B u_k$$

**Equation 2 — A Priori Error Covariance:**
$$P_{k|k-1} = A P_{k-1|k-1} A^T + Q$$

**UPDATE STAGE (Measurement Update):**

**Equation 3 — Kalman Gain:**
$$K_k = P_{k|k-1} H^T (H P_{k|k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

**Equation 4 — A Posteriori State Estimate:**
$$\hat{x}_{k|k} = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k|k-1})$$

**Equation 5 — A Posteriori Error Covariance:**
$$P_{k|k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k|k-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **How $K_k$ Works Intuitively:**
> - If measurement noise $R \to 0$, then $K_k \to H^{-1}$: we **fully trust the measurement**.
> - If process noise $Q \to 0$, then $P_{k|k-1} \to 0$, so $K_k \to 0$: we **fully trust the prediction**.
> - $K_k$ optimally balances these two uncertainties.

---

### 2.2 Mean Shift Algorithm — Theoretical Foundation

The Mean Shift algorithm is a **non-parametric** technique for locating the **modes (peaks)** of a probability density function (PDF) defined over a feature space. In tracking, the PDF is typically a **color histogram** representing the target's appearance.

#### 2.2.1 Core Mathematical Idea

Given $n$ data points $x_i$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with weights $w_i$, the **kernel density estimate** is:

$$\hat{f}(x) = C \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, k\!\left(\left\| \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\|^2\right)$$

Where:
- $k(\cdot)$ = **Kernel function** (typically a Gaussian or Epanechnikov kernel)
- $h$ = **Bandwidth parameter** (window radius)
- $C$ = **Normalization constant**
- $w_i$ = **Weight** of data point $x_i$

#### 2.2.2 The Mean Shift Vector

Taking the **gradient** of $\hat{f}(x)$ and setting it to zero gives the **Mean Shift vector**:

$$m(x) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, w_i \, g\!\left(\left\| \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\|^2\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, g\!\left(\left\| \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\|^2\right)} - x$$

Where $g(x) = -k'(x)$ is the **derivative of the kernel profile**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mean Shift Iteration Rule:**
> $$x_{k+1} = x_k + m(x_k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, w_i \, g\!\left(\left\| \frac{x_k - x_i}{h} \right\|^2\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, g\!\left(\left\| \frac{x_k - x_i}{h} \right\|^2\right)}$$
> 
> This is interpreted as: **"shift $x$ to the weighted centroid of the kernel window"**. The iteration converges to the nearest mode of the PDF.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Purpose | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Transition | $x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_{k-1}$ | Predict next state | State-units |
| 2 | Measurement | $z_k = H x_k + v_k$ | Sensor observation | Measurement-units |
| 3 | A Priori State | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B u_k$ | Predicted state | State-units |
| 4 | A Priori Covariance | $P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^T + Q$ | Uncertainty before measurement | State-units² |
| 5 | Kalman Gain | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Optimal weight | Dimensionless |
| 6 | A Posteriori State | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$ | Updated estimate | State-units |
| 7 | A Posteriori Covariance | $P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1}$ | Uncertainty after measurement | State-units² |
| 8 | Mean Shift Vector | $m(x) = \frac{\sum x_i w_i g(\cdot)}{\sum w_i g(\cdot)} - x$ | Direction of ascent | Pixels |
| 9 | Gaussian Kernel | $K(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}h} e^{-\frac{x^2}{2h^2}}$ | Smooth weighting | Probability density |
| 10 | Epanechnikov Kernel | $K(x) = \frac{3}{4}(1 - x^2)$ for $\vert x \vert \leq 1$ | Optimal MSE kernel | Probability density |
| 11 | Bhattacharyya Coefficient | $\rho(p, q) = \sum_{u=1}^{m} \sqrt{p_u q_u}$ | Target-model similarity | $\rho \in [0, 1]$ |
| 12 | Process Noise | $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ | Model uncertainty | Covariance matrix |
| 13 | Measurement Noise | $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ | Sensor noise | Covariance matrix |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of these equations:**
> - Equation 3-5 are deployed in **autonomous drone navigation** to fuse GPS, IMU, and odometry.
> - Equation 11 (Bhattacharyya Coefficient) is the **similarity metric** used in Mean Shift tracking to compare the target's color histogram in the current frame with the reference histogram.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Update Equations

We seek the estimator $\hat{x}_{k\vert k}$ that minimizes the **a posteriori error covariance**:

$$P_{k\vert k} = E\!\left[(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k})(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k})^T\right]$$

The estimator takes the form (intuitive: predicted state + correction from innovation):

$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$$

Substitute $z_k = H x_k + v_k$ into the measurement residual (called the **innovation**):

$$\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = H x_k + v_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = H(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}) + v_k$$

The error is $e_k = x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_k &= x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k} \\
    &= x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} - K_k (H x_k + v_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}) \\
    &= (I - K_k H)(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}) - K_k v_k
\end{aligned}$$

The a posteriori error covariance is:

$$P_{k\vert k} = E[e_k \, e_k^T]$$

Since $E[(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}) v_k^T] = 0$ (uncorrelated process and measurement noise):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{k\vert k} &= (I - K_k H) E[(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})^T] (I - K_k H)^T + K_k E[v_k v_k^T] K_k^T \\
            &= (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1} (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R K_k^T
\end{aligned}$$

To minimize $\text{tr}(P_{k\vert k})$, take the derivative with respect to $K_k$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \text{tr}(P_{k\vert k})}{\partial K_k} = -2 (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + 2 K_k R = 0$$

Solving for $K_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1} H^T &= K_k R \\
P_{k\vert k-1} H^T - K_k H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T &= K_k R \\
P_{k\vert k-1} H^T &= K_k (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R) \\
K_k &= P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Example: Kalman Filter on a 1-D Moving Object

**Setup:** A car moves along a 1-D track with constant velocity. We track position $p$ and velocity $v$.

**State Vector:** $x_k = \begin{bmatrix} p_k \\ v_k \end{bmatrix}$

**System Matrices** (with $\Delta t = 1$ s):

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = [1]$$

**Initial Conditions:**
$$\hat{x}_{0\vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad P_{0\vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Measured value at $k=1$:** $z_1 = 1.2$ m (true position: $1.0$ m)

---

**Step 1 — PREDICT:**

$$\hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = A \hat{x}_{0\vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1\vert 0} &= A P_{0\vert 0} A^T + Q \\
             &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \\
             &= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — KALMAN GAIN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= P_{1\vert 0} H^T (H P_{1\vert 0} H^T + R)^{-1} \\
    &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} [1] \left( [1] \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + [1] \right)^{-1} \\
    &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} (2.1 + 1)^{-1} \\
    &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{3.1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6774 \\ 0.3226 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — UPDATE (Innovation = $z_1 - H\hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = 1.2 - 0 = 1.2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} &= \hat{x}_{1\vert 0} + K_1 (z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1\vert 0}) \\
                   &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.6774 \\ 0.3226 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 1.2 \\
                   &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.8129 \\ 0.3871 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1\vert 1} &= (I - K_1 H) P_{1\vert 0} \\
             &= \left( \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.6774 \\ 0.3226 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \right) \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \\
             &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.3226 & 0 \\ -0.3226 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6774 & 0.3226 \\ 0.3226 & 0.7871 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Result:**
> - The Kalman Filter estimated the car's position as $0.8129$ m — closer to the true value ($1.0$ m) than the noisy measurement ($1.2$ m) and certainly more accurate than the prediction ($0$ m).
> - The Kalman Gain $K_1 = [0.6774, 0.3226]^T$ is **less than 1**, meaning we trust the prediction more than the noisy measurement.
> - The reduced covariance $P_{1\vert 1}$ reflects reduced uncertainty after incorporating the measurement.

---

### 3.3 Mean Shift — Iterative Mode-Seeking Numerical Example

**Setup:** We have 5 data points on a 1-D line at positions $x_i = \{2, 3, 8, 9, 10\}$ with weights $w_i = 1$ and a Gaussian kernel of bandwidth $h = 2.5$.

**Initial seed point:** $x_0 = 5$

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(x_0) &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} x_i \, w_i \, g(x_0 - x_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \, g(x_0 - x_i)} - x_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $g(x) = \exp(-x^2 / 2h^2) = \exp(-x^2 / 12.5)$ for the Gaussian profile.

Compute $g(x_0 - x_i)$ for each $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(5 - 2) &= \exp(-9/12.5) = \exp(-0.72) = 0.4868 \\
g(5 - 3) &= \exp(-4/12.5) = \exp(-0.32) = 0.7261 \\
g(5 - 8) &= \exp(-9/12.5) = \exp(-0.72) = 0.4868 \\
g(5 - 9) &= \exp(-16/12.5) = \exp(-1.28) = 0.2780 \\
g(5 - 10) &= \exp(-25/12.5) = \exp(-2.00) = 0.1353
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerator: 
$$2(0.4868) + 3(0.7261) + 8(0.4868) + 9(0.2780) + 10(0.1353) = 0.9736 + 2.1783 + 3.8944 + 2.5020 + 1.3530 = 10.9013$$

Denominator: 
$$0.4868 + 0.7261 + 0.4868 + 0.2780 + 0.1353 = 2.1130$$

New centroid: 
$$x_1 = \frac{10.9013}{2.1130} = 5.1594$$

Mean shift vector: $m(x_0) = 5.1594 - 5.0 = 0.1594$

**Iteration 2 (similar procedure, $x_1 = 5.1594$):**

After recomputation, the new centroid converges to $x_2 \approx 5.21$ (a positive shift, as expected — moving toward the cluster $\{8, 9, 10\}$).

**Iteration 3 and beyond:** The shift magnitude $\vert m(x_k) \vert$ decreases as we approach the mode, and the algorithm converges near $x \approx 5.4$ (a local mode between the two clusters).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Mean Shift's iterative centroid-shift converges to the nearest mode. The convergence is **guaranteed** (the sequence is monotonically increasing in density) but the result depends on the initial seed point.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Kalman Filter — Predict-Update Cycle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State x_prev, P_prev] --> B[Predict Stage]
    B --> C[Compute A Priori State: x_pred = A*x_prev + B*u]
    C --> D[Compute A Priori Covariance: P_pred = A*P_prev*A^T + Q]
    D --> E[Output Predicted State x_pred, P_pred]
    E --> F[Measurement Arrives: z_k]
    F --> G[Update Stage]
    G --> H[Compute Kalman Gain: K = P_pred*H^T / H*P_pred*H^T + R inverse]
    H --> I[Compute Innovation: y = z - H*x_pred]
    I --> J[Compute A Posteriori State: x_new = x_pred + K*y]
    J --> K[Compute A Posteriori Covariance: P_new = I - K*H * P_pred]
    K --> L[Output Updated State x_new, P_new]
    L --> M{Next Frame k plus 1?}
    M -- Yes --> B
    M -- No --> N[Final State Estimate]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style G fill:#ffe1e1
    style N fill:#d4f5d4
    style H fill:#ffe1ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is the canonical Kalman Filter loop. The blue box is initialization, the orange box is the **predict** (time-update) phase, and the red box is the **update** (measurement-update) phase. The loop recurses for every new frame in a video stream.

---

### 4.2 Mean Shift Tracking — Iterative Mode-Seeking Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Previous Frame Target Location y_0] --> B[Compute Target Histogram q in Initial Frame]
    B --> C[In New Frame: Initialize y_1 = y_0]
    C --> D[Compute Candidate Histogram p in Current Location]
    D --> E[Compute Bhattacharyya Coefficient rho between p and q]
    E --> F[Derive Weights w_i for Each Pixel]
    F --> G[Compute Weighted Centroid y_new]
    G --> H[Shift: y_1 = y_new]
    H --> I{Convergence Check: Shift less than Epsilon?}
    I -- No --> D
    I -- Yes --> J[Output New Target Location y_1]
    J --> K[Update Target Histogram q with new appearance]
    K --> L{Next Frame?}
    L -- Yes --> C
    L -- No --> M[Tracking Complete]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#ffe1ff
    style G fill:#e1ffe1
    style J fill:#d4f5d4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The yellow box is the **target model initialization**, the green box is the **mean shift centroid computation** (the heart of the algorithm), and the pink box is the **similarity check** (Bhattacharyya coefficient). The loop terminates when the shift is below a threshold $\epsilon$.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Architecture: Kalman Filter vs. Mean Shift

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KALMAN[Kalman Filter Paradigm]
        K1[Linear System Model Required] --> K2[State Space: position, velocity, etc.]
        K2 --> K3[Recursive Bayesian Estimation]
        K3 --> K4[Optimal for Gaussian Linear Systems]
        K4 --> K5[Output: Continuous State Estimate]
    end

    subgraph MEANSHIFT[Mean Shift Paradigm]
        M1[No Prior Model Required] --> M2[Feature Space: color histogram, etc.]
        M2 --> M3[Non-Parametric Density Estimation]
        M3 --> M4[Mode-Seeking on Probability Surface]
        M4 --> M5[Output: Local Mode Location]
    end

    KALMAN -.Versus.- MEANSHIFT

    style K1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style M1 fill:#e1e1ff
    style K5 fill:#d4f5d4
    style M5 fill:#d4f5d4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** The Kalman Filter is **model-driven** (requires $A, H, Q, R$ to be known) and produces **continuous-valued** state estimates. Mean Shift is **data-driven** (no model needed) and produces **discrete mode locations** of a feature distribution. KTU examiners often ask: "Compare and contrast the two approaches."

---

### 4.4 Mean Shift Vector Geometry (Conceptual Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Position x_k] --> B[Kernel Window Centered at x_k]
    B --> C[Sample Points x_i with Weights w_i]
    C --> D[Weighted Centroid Computation]
    D --> E[Mean Shift Vector m_x_k points from x_k to centroid]
    E --> F[New Position x_k_plus_1 equals centroid]
    F --> G{Converged?}
    G -- No --> A
    G -- Yes --> H[Mode Located]

    style E fill:#ffe1ff
    style H fill:#d4f5d4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometry Insight:** Each iteration of Mean Shift moves the kernel window from $x_k$ to the **weighted centroid** of the data points currently inside the window. The "shift" is literally the vector $m(x_k)$ pointing from the old center to the new center. The window always moves **uphill** on the density surface.
