## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Kalman Filter. List its TWO main stages and the FIVE core equations.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Kalman Filter** is an optimal recursive Bayesian estimator that estimates the state of a linear dynamic system from a series of noisy measurements. It operates under two assumptions: (i) the system is linear, and (ii) the process and measurement noise are Gaussian with zero mean.

**Two Stages:**
1. **Predict (Time-Update)** — Projects the current state forward in time.
2. **Update (Measurement-Update)** — Corrects the prediction using the new measurement.

**Five Core Equations:**

| # | Equation | Name |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B u_k$ | A priori state estimate |
| 2 | $P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^T + Q$ | A priori error covariance |
| 3 | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| 4 | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$ | A posteriori state estimate |
| 5 | $P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1}$ | A posteriori error covariance |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the Mean Shift algorithm. What is the role of the kernel in tracking?**

**Model Answer:**

**Mean Shift** is a non-parametric iterative algorithm that locates the **modes (peaks)** of a probability density function (PDF) representing the target object's appearance in feature space (e.g., color histogram).

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Choose a **kernel window** (typically Gaussian or Epanechnikov) centered at an initial seed location.
2. Compute the **weighted centroid** of all data points within the window.
3. **Shift** the kernel window to the new centroid.
4. Repeat steps 2-3 until the shift is less than a threshold $\epsilon$ (convergence).

**Role of the Kernel in Tracking:**
- The kernel assigns **higher weights to points closer to the window center** and lower weights to distant points.
- The **bandwidth $h$** controls the window size: a small $h$ gives fine-grained tracking but is sensitive to noise; a large $h$ is robust but may miss the exact object boundary.
- The kernel makes the algorithm **robust to outliers** and **invariant to small perturbations** in the data, making it ideal for real-time video tracking.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the Kalman Gain equation. Show the role of the Kalman Gain in balancing the prediction and measurement updates.** (7 Marks)

**(b) A 1-D vehicle tracking system uses a Kalman Filter. The state is $x = [p, v]^T$. The system matrices are $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}$, $R = [0.5]$. Initial conditions: $\hat{x}_0 = [0, 0]^T$, $P_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. At $k=1$, measurement $z_1 = 0.9$ m is received. Compute $\hat{x}_{1\vert 1}$ and $P_{1\vert 1}$.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Kalman Gain:**

Starting from the a posteriori error covariance:
$$P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1} (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R K_k^T$$

Expanding:
$$P_{k\vert k} = P_{k\vert k-1} - K_k H P_{k\vert k-1} - P_{k\vert k-1} H^T K_k^T + K_k (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R) K_k^T$$

Substitute the definition of $K_k$ to simplify. To minimize the trace $\text{tr}(P_{k\vert k})$, differentiate with respect to $K_k$:

$$\frac{\partial \text{tr}(P_{k\vert k})}{\partial K_k} = -2 P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + 2 K_k (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R) = 0$$

Solving for $K_k$:
$$K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

**Role of Kalman Gain:**
- When $R$ is small (sensor is reliable), $K_k$ is large, and we trust the measurement more.
- When $Q$ is small (model is reliable), $K_k$ is small, and we trust the prediction more.
- $K_k$ **optimally blends** the model and the measurement to minimize the mean-squared error.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Predict:**

$$\hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = A \hat{x}_{0\vert 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1\vert 0} &= A P_0 A^T + Q \\
             &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} \\
             &= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Kalman Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H P_{1\vert 0} H^T &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 2.01 \\
K_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 \\ 1.00 \end{bmatrix} (2.01 + 0.5)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 \\ 1.00 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2.51} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8008 \\ 0.3984 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Update (Innovation = $0.9 - 0 = 0.9$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.8008 \\ 0.3984 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.9 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7207 \\ 0.3586 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1\vert 1} &= (I - K_1 H) P_{1\vert 0} \\
             &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.1992 & 0 \\ -0.3984 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} \\
             &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.4004 & 0.1992 \\ 0.1992 & 0.6117 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} = [0.7207, 0.3586]^T$ m, $P_{1\vert 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4004 & 0.1992 \\ 0.1992 & 0.6117 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Mean Shift tracking algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of the Bhattacharyya coefficient in target localization.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Given five 2-D data points $(2, 3)$, $(3, 4)$, $(8, 9)$, $(9, 10)$, $(10, 11)$ with equal weights, a Gaussian kernel of bandwidth $h = 3$, and initial seed $x_0 = (5, 5)$, perform ONE iteration of the Mean Shift algorithm and compute the new centroid.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mean Shift Tracking Algorithm — Detailed Explanation:**

Mean Shift tracking consists of the following steps:

1. **Target Model Initialization:** In the first frame, define a rectangular region around the target. Compute its **color histogram** (or feature histogram) $q = \{q_u\}_{u=1}^m$ using a kernel $k$ to assign higher weights to pixels near the center.

2. **Candidate Model:** In each new frame, compute the histogram $p(y) = \{p_u(y)\}_{u=1}^m$ of the candidate region centered at location $y$.

3. **Similarity Measurement:** Compute the **Bhattacharyya coefficient**:
$$\rho(p(y), q) = \sum_{u=1}^{m} \sqrt{p_u(y) \, q_u}$$

A value of $\rho$ close to 1 indicates high similarity to the target.

4. **Weight Computation:** Derive weights for each pixel:
$$w_i = \sum_{u=1}^{m} \sqrt{\frac{q_u}{p_u(y_0)}} \, \delta(b(x_i) - u)$$

where $b(x_i)$ is the bin index of pixel $x_i$.

5. **Mean Shift Iteration:** Shift the target location to the weighted centroid:
$$y_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n_h} x_i \, w_i \, g\!\left(\left\| \frac{y_0 - x_i}{h} \right\|^2\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n_h} w_i \, g\!\left(\left\| \frac{y_0 - x_i}{h} \right\|^2\right)}$$

6. **Convergence:** Repeat until $\vert \rho(p(y_1), q) - \rho(p(y_0), q) \vert < \epsilon$.

**Role of Bhattacharyya Coefficient:**
- It provides a **statistical similarity measure** between two histograms in the range $[0, 1]$.
- It is **scale-invariant** and **robust to partial occlusions**.
- It guides the mean shift iteration by quantifying how close the candidate is to the target model — the algorithm seeks to **maximize $\rho$**.

---

**(b) One Iteration of Mean Shift:**

Kernel: $g(x) = \exp(-x^2 / 2h^2) = \exp(-x^2 / 18)$ for $h = 3$.

Squared distances from $x_0 = (5, 5)$ to each point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1^2 &= (5-2)^2 + (5-3)^2 = 9 + 4 = 13 \\
d_2^2 &= (5-3)^2 + (5-4)^2 = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
d_3^2 &= (5-8)^2 + (5-9)^2 = 9 + 16 = 25 \\
d_4^2 &= (5-9)^2 + (5-10)^2 = 16 + 25 = 41 \\
d_5^2 &= (5-10)^2 + (5-11)^2 = 25 + 36 = 61
\end{aligned}
$$

Kernel weights:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1 &= \exp(-13/18) = \exp(-0.7222) = 0.4857 \\
g_2 &= \exp(-5/18) = \exp(-0.2778) = 0.7575 \\
g_3 &= \exp(-25/18) = \exp(-1.3889) = 0.2494 \\
g_4 &= \exp(-41/18) = \exp(-2.2778) = 0.1027 \\
g_5 &= \exp(-61/18) = \exp(-3.3889) = 0.0337
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerator (x-component):**
$$2(0.4857) + 3(0.7575) + 8(0.2494) + 9(0.1027) + 10(0.0337) = 0.9714 + 2.2725 + 1.9952 + 0.9243 + 0.3370 = 6.5004$$

**Numerator (y-component):**
$$3(0.4857) + 4(0.7575) + 9(0.2494) + 10(0.1027) + 11(0.0337) = 1.4571 + 3.0300 + 2.2446 + 1.0270 + 0.3707 = 8.1294$$

**Denominator:**
$$0.4857 + 0.7575 + 0.2494 + 0.1027 + 0.0337 = 1.6290$$

**New Centroid:**
$$x_1 = \left( \frac{6.5004}{1.6290}, \frac{8.1294}{1.6290} \right) = (3.9904, 4.9907)$$

**Mean Shift Vector:** $m(x_0) = (3.9904 - 5, 4.9907 - 5) = (-1.0096, -0.0093)$. The negative $x$-shift is unexpected — but in a single iteration the algorithm is biased toward the closer cluster $(2, 3), (3, 4)$. Subsequent iterations would continue shifting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $P_{k\vert k-1}$ with $P_{k-1\vert k-1}$:** The a priori covariance (predicted) is *different* from the a posteriori covariance of the previous step. Mixing these up costs **3 marks** in derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting $Q$ and $R$ units:** Process noise $Q$ has units of *state-units²*, while $R$ has units of *measurement-units²*. Examiners may deduct marks for dimensionally inconsistent noise matrices.
> 3. **Mean Shift seed-dependence:** Students often claim Mean Shift "always converges to the global maximum." This is **false** — it converges to the *nearest mode* from the initial seed. A global solution requires a multi-seed or coarse-to-fine approach.
> 4. **Skipping the kernel computation steps in numerical problems:** The kernel function value $g(x_i)$ must be **explicitly evaluated** for each data point. Writing only the final formula without intermediate values loses **2-3 marks**.
> 5. **Matrix multiplication errors in Kalman Filter:** The product $A P A^T$ is non-commutative. Students frequently write $P A^T A$ or $A^2 P$, which are wrong. Always multiply in the correct order: $A \cdot P \cdot A^T$.
> 6. **Confusing Kalman Filter with particle filters:** The KF is optimal only for **linear Gaussian** systems. For non-linear systems, an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** is required. Mentioning this distinction can earn **bonus marks** in KTU answers.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Kalman Filter = 2 stages (Predict, Update) + 5 equations** — Memorize the order: (1) A priori state, (2) A priori covariance, (3) Kalman gain, (4) A posteriori state, (5) A posteriori covariance.
- The Kalman Filter is **optimal** only for **linear** systems with **Gaussian** noise. For non-linear systems, use EKF, UKF, or Particle Filters.
- $K_k$ is dimensionless; $P, Q$ have units of *state²*; $R$ has units of *measurement²*; $A, H$ are pure matrices.
- The Kalman Filter **never discards** past data — it propagates $\hat{x}$ and $P$ recursively, giving $O(1)$ memory per step.
- **Mean Shift = iterative mode-seeker** with no model assumption and no prior training required.
- The Mean Shift convergence is **guaranteed** but **seed-dependent** (local mode, not global).
- **Bhattacharyya coefficient** $\rho \in [0, 1]$ measures histogram similarity and drives Mean Shift tracking.
- **Gaussian kernel** is the most common in tracking; **Epanechnikov kernel** is optimal in the MSE sense.
- **Bandwidth $h$**: too small = noisy tracking; too large = slow convergence, may miss the target.
- **Comparison point for KTU:** KF is *parametric & model-based*; Mean Shift is *non-parametric & data-driven*.
- **Hybrid trackers** (KF + Mean Shift) use Mean Shift for coarse localization and KF for smooth trajectory prediction — this is a **frequently asked KTU question**.
- Key limitations: KF struggles with **non-Gaussian noise**; Mean Shift struggles with **fast motion** (the kernel cannot follow if the target jumps between frames).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Stereo Vision, Epipolar Geometry, Disparity Maps, Depth Estimation, Structure from Motion (SfM), Camera Calibration, and 3D Reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Stereo Vision, Epipolar Geometry, Disparity Maps, Depth Estimation, Structure from Motion (SfM), Camera Calibration, and 3D Reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stereo Vision?

**Stereo Vision (Stereopsis)** is a sub-domain of computer vision that mimics the biological binocular vision system by recovering the three-dimensional (3D) geometric structure of a scene from two or more two-dimensional (2D) images captured from different viewpoints. The underlying principle is **triangulation**: given the projection of the same 3D point in two camera views with a known geometric relationship, the 3D location of that point can be uniquely determined.

Mathematically, stereo vision is the inverse problem of the **pinhole image formation model**. While the forward model projects 3D world coordinates onto 2D image coordinates (a many-to-one mapping), stereo vision attempts to invert this projection using the additional constraint provided by a second (or more) viewpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Stereo vision is the process of inferring depth information of a scene by comparing two or more images taken from slightly different positions, exploiting the principle of triangulation analogous to human binocular vision.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Two-Eyed Human Visual System

Consider how humans perceive depth. Each eye captures a slightly different image of the world because the eyes are separated by approximately **6.5 cm** (the *interocular distance* or *baseline*). The brain fuses these two retinal images in the **visual cortex** through a process called **stereoscopic fusion**, and the small horizontal offset between corresponding points in the two retinal images — known as **retinal disparity** — is the brain's primary cue for depth perception.

A simple home experiment confirms this: hold a finger in front of your face and alternately close each eye. The finger appears to "jump" horizontally against the background. This horizontal jump is the **disparity**, and it is inversely proportional to the finger's distance from your face. The closer the finger, the larger the disparity; the farther, the smaller the disparity.

Computer stereo vision replicates this exact mechanism using two cameras separated by a precisely known *baseline*, calculating the disparity of corresponding pixels mathematically to obtain a **depth map**.

## 1.3 Core Concepts in Stereo Vision

The following terminologies are central to Module 4 and appear frequently in KTU examination questions:

| Concept | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Baseline (B)** | The physical distance between the optical centers of the two cameras. |
| **Disparity (d)** | The horizontal pixel offset between corresponding points in the left and right images: $d = u_L - u_R$. |
| **Depth (Z)** | The perpendicular distance from the camera's principal plane to the 3D point. |
| **Focal Length (f)** | The distance from the camera's optical center to the image plane, measured in pixels as $f_x$ and $f_y$. |
| **Principal Point** | The image coordinate where the optical axis intersects the image plane: $(c_x, c_y)$. |
| **Correspondence Problem** | The challenge of identifying which pixel in the right image corresponds to a given pixel in the left image. |

## 1.4 Why Stereo Vision Matters in Engineering

Stereo vision is the foundation of countless real-world applications: autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo) use stereo rigs for obstacle detection; industrial robots perform bin-picking using depth from stereo; medical imaging systems reconstruct 3D anatomical structures from endoscopic video; aerial photogrammetry builds 3D terrain models from overlapping aerial images; and AR/VR headsets (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro) use stereo cameras to blend virtual objects with the real world. In all these systems, the depth equation $Z = \frac{fB}{d}$ is the mathematical heart of the technology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus Point:** In Module 4, the *most examinable* relation is the **disparity-to-depth equation** $Z = \frac{fB}{d}$ and the **fundamental matrix epipolar constraint** $p_R^T F p_L = 0$. Master these two and you master the module's core.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pinhole Camera Model — Foundation of All Vision Geometry

Every camera in computer vision is approximated as a **pinhole camera**, where light from a 3D scene point $P = (X, Y, Z)^T$ passes through an infinitesimally small aperture (the optical center $O$) and is projected onto an image plane at distance $f$ (the focal length) behind the aperture. Under perspective projection:

$$x = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$$

This can be expressed in homogeneous coordinates using the **camera matrix** $K$ (also called the **calibration matrix** or **intrinsic matrix**):

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $f_x, f_y$ are the focal lengths in pixel units, $(c_x, c_y)$ is the principal point, and $s$ is the **skew parameter** (typically zero for modern cameras).

## 2.2 The Full Camera Projection Matrix

A 3D world point $P_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$ (in homogeneous coordinates) is mapped to an image point $p = (u, v, 1)^T$ through:

$$p \sim K \cdot [R \mid t] \cdot P_w$$

The $3 \times 4$ matrix $P = K[R \mid t]$ is called the **camera projection matrix**. It encapsulates:
- **Intrinsic parameters** ($K$): internal to the camera (focal length, principal point, skew).
- **Extrinsic parameters** ($R, t$): the camera's position and orientation in the world frame, where $R$ is a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix ($R \in SO(3)$) and $t$ is a $3 \times 1$ translation vector.

## 2.3 Stereo Vision Geometry — Two Cameras, One Scene

Consider a **canonical stereo configuration**:
- **Left camera** at the origin of the world frame: projection matrix $P_L = K_L[I \mid 0]$.
- **Right camera** translated by baseline $B$ along the X-axis: projection matrix $P_R = K_R[R \mid t]$, where $R = I$ and $t = (B, 0, 0)^T$ for the simplest case.

Assuming identical, rectified cameras ($K_L = K_R = K$, no rotation), a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to:
- Left image: $u_L = f \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$
- Right image: $u_R = f \frac{X - B}{Z} + c_x$

The **disparity** $d = u_L - u_R = \frac{fB}{Z}$. Inverting gives the **depth equation**:

$$Z = \frac{fB}{d}$$

## 2.4 Epipolar Geometry — The Geometric Constraint of Two Views

**Epipolar geometry** describes the geometric relationship between two cameras observing the same 3D scene. It captures the fact that for any point in one image, its corresponding point in the other image must lie on a specific line — the **epipolar line**. This reduces the correspondence search from a 2D problem to a 1D problem.

The geometry is defined by:
- **Epipoles** ($e_L, e_R$): the projections of one camera's optical center into the other camera's image plane.
- **Epipolar planes**: the plane containing the two camera centers and the 3D point $P$.
- **Epipolar lines** ($l_L, l_R$): the intersections of the epipolar plane with the two image planes.

### The Essential Matrix ($E$)

For **calibrated cameras**, the constraint between corresponding image points $p_L$ and $p_R$ (in *normalized image coordinates*) is captured by the **essential matrix**:

$$p_R^T E p_L = 0, \quad E = [t]_\times R$$

where $[t]_\times$ is the **skew-symmetric matrix** of the translation vector $t$:

$$[t]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The essential matrix has rank 2 and has exactly **5 degrees of freedom** (3 for $R$, 2 for $t$ up to scale, since $t$ is recoverable only up to scale from two views).

### The Fundamental Matrix ($F$)

For **uncalibrated cameras** (raw pixel coordinates), the equivalent constraint uses the **fundamental matrix**:

$$p_R^T F p_L = 0, \quad F = K_R^{-T} E K_L^{-1} = K_R^{-T} [t]_\times R K_L^{-1}$$

The fundamental matrix has **7 degrees of freedom** (rank 2, $3 \times 3$ with 9 entries minus 1 for scale minus 1 for the rank-2 determinant condition).

### Recovering $R$ and $t$ from $E$

Given $E$, the rotation and translation can be recovered via **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)**. Let $E = U \Sigma V^T$, then:

$$R = U W V^T \text{ or } U W^T V^T, \quad t = \pm u_3$$

where $W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $u_3$ is the third column of $U$. This yields **four possible configurations** ($R, t$), only one of which places the 3D point in front of both cameras (the **cheirality check**).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** cheat sheet for the KTU University Exam. Master every row.

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Perspective projection (pinhole) | $x = fX/Z, \quad y = fY/Z$ | $f$ in mm or pixels |
| 2 | Camera matrix (intrinsic) | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 3 | Full projection equation | $p \sim K[R \mid t] P_w$ | Homogeneous, $\sim$ denotes equality up to scale |
| 4 | **Disparity** | $d = u_L - u_R$ | Pixels; always $\geq 0$ for rectified stereo |
| 5 | **Depth from disparity** | $Z = fB / d$ | $B$ in same units as $f$; $Z$ in those units |
| 6 | Depth resolution | $\Delta Z = Z^2 / (fB)$ | Inverse quadratic: error grows with $Z^2$ |
| 7 | Essential matrix definition | $E = [t]_\times R$ | Calibrated; 5 DOF; rank 2 |
| 8 | Fundamental matrix definition | $F = K^{-T} E K^{-1}$ | Uncalibrated; 7 DOF; rank 2 |
| 9 | **Epipolar constraint** | $p_R^T F p_L = 0$ | Scalar equation, $\in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 10 | Epipolar line in right image | $l_R = F p_L$ | Line equation: $l_R = [a, b, c]^T$ |
| 11 | Skew-symmetric operator | $[t]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $[t]_\times v = t \times v$ |
| 12 | Triangulation (mid-point) | $P = \frac{1}{2}(P_{left} + P_{right})$ | 3D point from two rays |
| 13 | Calibration reprojection error | $\min \sum_i \vert\vert p_i - \hat{p}_i \vert\vert^2$ | $\hat{p}_i$ is the observed image point |
| 14 | Bundle adjustment objective | $\min_{P_j, X_i} \sum_{i,j} \vert\vert \pi(P_j, X_i) - x_{ij} \vert\vert^2$ | Joint optimization over cameras and points |
| 15 | Zhang's planar constraint | $h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0$ | $h_1, h_2$ are first two columns of homography |
| 16 | Focal length from Zhang | $f_x = \sqrt{\lambda / B_{11}}, \quad f_y = \sqrt{\lambda B_{11} / (B_{11}B_{22} - B_{12}^2)}$ | $\lambda$ is the scale factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** In the formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value or norm as appropriate. The **vertical bar** has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` in any LaTeX-rendered row to maintain the markdown table structure (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safety protocols).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas in the cheat sheet are not abstract — they are the daily working tools of:
- **Autonomous driving engineers** at companies like Waymo and Cruise, who calibrate vehicle-mounted stereo rigs using Zhang's method and then run real-time block-matching algorithms to compute $Z = fB/d$ for every pixel at 30+ FPS.
- **Aerial surveyors** who use SfM pipelines (e.g., COLMAP, OpenSfM) on drone imagery to reconstruct city-scale 3D models.
- **Medical device engineers** who calibrate endoscopic stereo cameras and use triangulation to measure lesion dimensions during surgery.
- **AR/VR developers** at Meta and Apple who perform simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) using epipolar geometry to estimate headset pose from environmental features.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Perspective Projection Equations

**Starting Principle:** The pinhole camera model assumes a small aperture $O$ at the origin of the camera coordinate system, with the image plane at distance $f$ along the $Z$-axis. A 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ emits (or reflects) a ray that passes through $O$ and strikes the image plane at $p = (x, y, -f)$ in the *camera* frame (note the negative sign for a real image).

**Step 1 — Similar Triangles:** Consider the triangle formed by $O$, $P$, and the Z-axis, and the similar triangle formed by $O$, $p$, and the Z-axis. By similar triangles:

$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}, \quad \frac{y}{f} = \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Image Coordinates:**

$$x = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Pixel Coordinates:** The image plane is sampled by a discrete sensor with pixel pitch $(\Delta_x, \Delta_y)$ and a principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$:

$$\begin{aligned} u &= \frac{x}{\Delta_x} + c_x = \frac{f}{\Delta_x} \frac{X}{Z} + c_x = f_x \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \\ v &= \frac{y}{\Delta_y} + c_y = \frac{f}{\Delta_y} \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y = f_y \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y \end{aligned}$$

where $f_x = f / \Delta_x$ and $f_y = f / \Delta_y$ are the focal lengths in pixel units.

**Step 4 — Homogeneous Form:** Stacking the equations and converting to homogeneous coordinates $(u, v, 1)^T \sim (uZ, vZ, Z)^T$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X/Z \\ Y/Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} / Z$$

**Step 5 — Including Extrinsics:** To project a *world-coordinate* point $P_w$ into the camera, apply rotation and translation first: $P_c = R P_w + t$. Hence:

$$p \sim K (R P_w + t) = K [R \mid t] P_w^{(h)}$$

where $P_w^{(h)} = (X, Y, Z, 1)^T$ is the world point in homogeneous coordinates.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Disparity-to-Depth Equation

**Setup:** A canonical rectified stereo pair has the left camera at the origin and the right camera translated by $(B, 0, 0)$. Both cameras share intrinsic matrix $K$ and have parallel optical axes.

**Step 1 — Projection of Point P into Left Camera:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} u_L \\ v_L \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} I \mid 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x X / Z + c_x \\ f_y Y / Z + c_y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $u_L = f_x X / Z + c_x$ and $v_L = f_y Y / Z + c_y$.

**Step 2 — Projection of Point P into Right Camera:** The right camera's optical center is at $(B, 0, 0)$, so the point's coordinate *relative to the right camera* is $(X - B, Y, Z)$. Projecting:

$$u_R = f_x \frac{X - B}{Z} + c_x, \quad v_R = f_y \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$$

**Step 3 — Compute Disparity:** Since the cameras are rectified, $v_L = v_R$ (the $v$ coordinates are equal), and:

$$d = u_L - u_R = \frac{f_x X}{Z} + c_x - \left( \frac{f_x (X - B)}{Z} + c_x \right) = \frac{f_x B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Depth:**

$$Z = \frac{f_x B}{d}$$

This is the **fundamental depth equation** of stereo vision. The same derivation with $f_y$ and $f_x = f_y = f$ (square pixels) yields the textbook form $Z = fB / d$.

**Step 5 — Engineering Insight — Depth Resolution:** Differentiating $Z = fB/d$ with respect to $d$:

$$\frac{dZ}{dd} = -\frac{fB}{d^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{fB}$$

This shows that **depth error grows quadratically with distance**. At $Z = 10$ meters, a 1-pixel disparity error gives a 10× larger depth error than at $Z = 1$ meter. This is why stereo systems have limited *useful range* and why long-range depth systems use other modalities (LiDAR, radar, ToF).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Essential Matrix from Epipolar Geometry

**Step 1 — Setup in Calibrated Coordinates:** Let $x_L$ and $x_R$ be the projections of a 3D point $P$ in normalized image coordinates (i.e., $x = K^{-1} p$). The rays from the cameras to $P$ are:

$$x_L = \frac{1}{Z_L}(X, Y, Z)^T, \quad x_R = \frac{1}{Z_R}(R P + t)$$

**Step 2 — Co-planarity Constraint:** The three points $O_L$, $O_R$, and $P$ lie in a single plane (the epipolar plane). The translation vector $t$ from $O_L$ to $O_R$ also lies in this plane. Therefore, the vectors $\overrightarrow{O_L P}$, $\overrightarrow{O_R P}$, and $t$ are coplanar. In vector algebra, three vectors are coplanar if and only if their **scalar triple product is zero**:

$$(P - O_L) \cdot (t \times (P - O_R)) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express in Normalized Coordinates:** Since $x_L \sim P - O_L$ and $x_R \sim P - O_R$ (both are scaled versions of the rays from the optical centers to $P$), the coplanarity condition becomes:

$$x_L \cdot (t \times x_R) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Use the Vector Triple Product Identity:** The expression $t \times x_R$ is a vector, and $x_L \cdot (t \times x_R)$ can be rewritten using the **scalar triple product identity**:

$$x_L \cdot (t \times x_R) = (x_L \times t) \cdot x_R = 0$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the Skew-Symmetric Matrix:** The cross product $t \times x_R$ can be written as matrix multiplication:

$$t \times x_R = [t]_\times x_R$$

Therefore:

$$x_L^T [t]_\times x_R = 0$$

**Step 6 — Define the Essential Matrix:** Let $E = [t]_\times R$. Since $x_R \sim R P + t$ and the cross product is invariant under rotation, the final constraint is:

$$x_L^T E x_R = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad x_R^T E^T x_L = 0$$

This is the celebrated **essential matrix constraint**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Fundamental Matrix from the Essential Matrix

**Step 1 — Relate Normalized to Pixel Coordinates:** The normalized image coordinates $x$ are related to the raw pixel coordinates $p$ by:

$$x = K^{-1} p$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Essential Constraint:** $x_L = K_L^{-1} p_L$ and $x_R = K_R^{-1} p_R$. Substituting into $x_L^T E x_R = 0$:

$$(K_L^{-1} p_L)^T E (K_R^{-1} p_R) = 0$$

$$p_L^T K_L^{-T} E K_R^{-1} p_R = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define the Fundamental Matrix:**

$$F = K_L^{-T} E K_R^{-1}$$

Hence $p_L^T F p_R = 0$ is the **fundamental matrix constraint** in raw pixel coordinates.

## 3.5 Derivation of Zhang's Camera Calibration (Planar Method)

Zhang's method (2000) is the de facto standard for camera calibration. It uses a planar checkerboard pattern observed from multiple (at least 3) viewpoints.

**Step 1 — Homography from Planar Pattern:** For a planar target lying on $Z = 0$ in the world frame, the projection simplifies to:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = H \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $H = K [r_1 \mid r_2 \mid t]$ is a $3 \times 3$ homography.

**Step 2 — Homography Decomposition:** Since $r_1$ and $r_2$ are orthonormal (columns of a rotation matrix), and $K$ is upper triangular:

$$H = \lambda K \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & t \end{bmatrix}$$

Let $H = \begin{bmatrix} h_1 & h_2 & h_3 \end{bmatrix}$. Then:

$$h_1 = \lambda K r_1, \quad h_2 = \lambda K r_2, \quad h_3 = \lambda K t$$

**Step 3 — Orthonormality Constraints:** From $r_1^T r_2 = 0$ and $\vert\vert r_1 \vert\vert = \vert\vert r_2 \vert\vert = 1$:

$$h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0 \quad \text{(orthogonality)}$$

$$h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_1 = h_2^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 \quad \text{(equal norm)}$$

**Step 4 — Define B Matrix:** Let $B = K^{-T} K^{-1}$ (a symmetric $3 \times 3$ matrix with 6 unknowns). The constraints become **linear** in the entries of $B$:

$$h_i^T B h_j = \begin{bmatrix} h_{i1} h_{j1} \\ h_{i1} h_{j2} + h_{i2} h_{j1} \\ h_{i2} h_{j2} \\ h_{i3} h_{j1} + h_{i1} h_{j3} \\ h_{i3} h_{j2} + h_{i2} h_{j3} \\ h_{i3} h_{j3} \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} \\ B_{12} \\ B_{22} \\ B_{13} \\ B_{23} \\ B_{33} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for B:** With $n \geq 3$ views, stacking these equations gives $V b = 0$ (where $b$ is the 6-vector of $B$ entries), which is solved via SVD of $V$. Then $B$ is decomposed (Cholesky) to obtain $K$.

**Step 6 — Recover Extrinsics:** Once $K$ is known, each homography gives $r_1 = \lambda K^{-1} h_1$, $r_2 = \lambda K^{-1} h_2$, $r_3 = r_1 \times r_2$, $t = \lambda K^{-1} h_3$ (with $\lambda = 1 / \vert\vert K^{-1} h_1 \vert\vert$).

**Step 7 — Refinement via Maximum Likelihood:** Minimize the **reprojection error** over all views and all calibration points:

$$\min_{K, R_i, t_i, X_j} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \vert\vert p_{ij} - \hat{p}(K, R_i, t_i, X_j) \vert\vert^2$$

This nonlinear optimization (Levenberg-Marquardt) is called the **maximum likelihood estimate**.

## 3.6 Triangulation — Recovering 3D from Two Views

Given corresponding image points $p_L$ and $p_R$ and the projection matrices $P_L, P_R$, the 3D point $P$ lies at the intersection of two rays. In practice, the rays do not intersect exactly due to noise, so we find the point closest to both rays (the **midpoint triangulation**).

**Step 1 — Form Projection Equations:** From $p_L \times (P_L P) = 0$ and $p_R \times (P_R P) = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u_L P_L^{3T} - P_L^{1T} \\ v_L P_L^{3T} - P_L^{2T} \\ u_R P_R^{3T} - P_R^{1T} \\ v_R P_R^{3T} - P_R^{2T} \end{bmatrix} P = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve the Homogeneous Linear System:** $A P = 0$ is solved via SVD; the solution is the right singular vector corresponding to the smallest singular value of $A$.

**Step 3 — Dehomogenize:** Divide by the 4th coordinate to obtain $P = (X, Y, Z, 1)^T \to (X, Y, Z)$.

## 3.7 Structure from Motion (SfM) — The Big Picture

**Structure from Motion** is the generalization of stereo vision to an arbitrary, unordered collection of images taken from different (unknown) viewpoints at different (unknown) times. The goal is to recover both the 3D structure (the "structure") and the camera poses (the "motion") simultaneously.

**The SfM Pipeline (analytical step matrix):**

1. **Feature Detection:** Extract salient keypoints (SIFT, SURF, ORB) from each image.
2. **Feature Matching:** Match keypoints across image pairs using descriptor distance (e.g., Lowe's ratio test).
3. **Geometric Verification:** Estimate $F$ (or $E$ after calibration) using RANSAC; reject outlier matches.
4. **Pose Initialization:** Recover $(R, t)$ from $E$ for an initial image pair; triangulate initial 3D points.
5. **Incremental Reconstruction:** Register new images via **PnP** (Perspective-n-Point) using 2D-3D correspondences, then triangulate new points.
6. **Bundle Adjustment:** Globally refine all camera poses and 3D points by minimizing the total reprojection error:

$$\min_{\{P_j\}, \{X_i\}} \sum_{i,j} \rho\left( \vert\vert \pi(P_j, X_i) - x_{ij} \vert\vert^2 \right)$$

where $\rho(\cdot)$ is a robust loss function (e.g., Huber) to downweight outliers.

7. **Multi-View Stereo (MVS) Densification:** Once sparse structure exists, compute dense depth maps per image using Patch-Match or plane-sweep stereo, then fuse into a final 3D point cloud or mesh.

```mermaid
        imgR["Image Plane Right"]
        projR["Projection Equation uR = f times X minus B divided by Z + cx"]

    subgraph COMPUTE["Depth Computation Engine"]
```

## 4.3 Full Structure-from-Motion (SfM) Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input["Input: Unordered Image Set I1 I2 ... In"]
    feat["Stage 1 Feature Detection: SIFT or ORB Keypoint Extraction"]
    match["Stage 2 Pairwise Feature Matching with Ratio Test"]
    ransac["Stage 3 Geometric Verification: RANSAC based F Matrix Estimation"]
    init["Stage 4 Two View Initialization: Recover R t and Triangulate Initial 3D Points"]
    reg["Stage 5 Incremental Camera Registration: PnP for New View Pose Estimation"]
    tri2["Stage 6 Triangulation of New 3D Points from Registered Views"]
    ba["Stage 7 Bundle Adjustment: Global Nonlinear Refinement Minimizing Reprojection Error"]
    mvs["Stage 8 Multi View Stereo Densification: Patch Match or Plane Sweep Stereo"]
    fuse["Stage 9 Depth Map Fusion into Dense 3D Point Cloud or Mesh"]
    output["Output: 3D Reconstruction with Camera Poses and Dense Geometry"]

    input --> feat
    feat --> match
    match --> ransac
    ransac --> init
    init --> reg
    reg --> tri2
    tri2 --> ba
    ba --> reg
    ba --> mvs
    mvs --> fuse
    fuse --> output
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    checker["Input: Multiple Images of Planar Checkerboard Pattern"]
    corner["Step 1: Corner Detection with Subpixel Refinement"]
    homog["Step 2: Per View Homography Estimation H from 2D to 2D Correspondences"]
    constr["Step 3: Assemble Orthonormality Constraints into Linear System Vb = 0"]
    solve["Step 4: Solve for B Matrix via SVD of V"]
    chol["Step 5: Cholesky Decomposition to Recover Intrinsic Matrix K"]
    extrin["Step 6: Compute Extrinsic R t for Each View from K and H"]
    refine["Step 7: Nonlinear Refinement Minimizing Reprojection Error via Levenberg Marquardt"]
    outK["Output: Camera Intrinsic Matrix K and Lens Distortion Coefficients"]
    outRT["Output: All Camera Poses R t"]

    checker --> corner
    corner --> homog
    homog --> constr
    constr --> solve
    solve --> chol
    chol --> extrin
    extrin --> refine
    refine --> outK

```


# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Define the term *disparity* in stereo vision. Derive the relationship between disparity and depth for a canonical rectified stereo setup.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Disparity is the horizontal pixel offset between the projections of the same 3D point in the left and right images of a stereo pair. Formally, for rectified cameras, $d = u_L - u_R$, where $u_L$ and $u_R$ are the $x$-coordinates (in pixels) of the corresponding points in the left and right images.

**Derivation (2 marks):** In a canonical rectified stereo system, the left camera is at the origin and the right camera is translated along the X-axis by baseline $B$. A 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to:

$$u_L = f \frac{X}{Z} + c_x, \quad u_R = f \frac{X - B}{Z} + c_x$$

Subtracting:

$$d = u_L - u_R = \frac{fB}{Z} \implies \boxed{Z = \frac{fB}{d}}$$

Hence, depth is **inversely proportional** to disparity.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — December 2022]

**What is the essential matrix? State its mathematical definition and the epipolar constraint it encodes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The essential matrix $E$ is a $3 \times 3$ rank-2 matrix that encodes the relative rotation $R$ and translation $t$ between two calibrated cameras. It is defined as:

$$E = [t]_\times R$$

where $[t]_\times$ is the skew-symmetric matrix of the translation vector:

$$[t]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Epipolar constraint (1 mark):** For any pair of corresponding points $x_L, x_R$ in *normalized image coordinates*:

$$\boxed{x_L^T E x_R = 0}$$

This scalar equation enforces that the two observation rays and the baseline are coplanar.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Module Internal Choice — Option A

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the pinhole camera model in detail. Derive the equations of perspective projection and show how the camera matrix $K$ (intrinsic parameters) is constructed. Define the extrinsic parameters and write the complete projection equation $p \sim K[R \mid t] P_w$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two cameras in a stereo rig are separated by a baseline $B = 200$ mm. The cameras have focal length $f = 800$ pixels, and their principal points are at $(c_x, c_y) = (320, 240)$. A 3D point projects to pixel coordinates $(u_L, v_L) = (412, 240)$ in the left image and $(u_R, v_R) = (388, 240)$ in the right image. Compute:
  (i) the disparity $d$,
  (ii) the depth $Z$,
  (iii) the 3D world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ of the point.

Assume a canonical rectified configuration and square pixels.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The pinhole camera models the image formation as a central projection through an infinitesimally small aperture. Let the camera's optical center be at the origin of a coordinate system, with the image plane at distance $f$ (focal length) along the $Z$-axis. A 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to image coordinates:

$$x = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$$

Converting to pixel coordinates using focal length in pixel units $f_x = f / \Delta_x$, $f_y = f / \Delta_y$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$:

$$u = f_x \frac{X}{Z} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$$

In homogeneous form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X/Z \\ Y/Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The **intrinsic matrix** is:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $s$ is the skew parameter (zero for most modern sensors).

The **extrinsic parameters** $R$ (3×3 rotation) and $t$ (3×1 translation) map world coordinates to camera coordinates: $P_c = R P_w + t$. The complete projection is:

$$\boxed{p \sim K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} P_w^{(h)} = K \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Disparity (2 marks):**

$$d = u_L - u_R = 412 - 388 = \mathbf{24 \text{ pixels}}$$

**(ii) Depth (3 marks):** Using $Z = fB / d$:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \times 200}{24} = \frac{160000}{24} \approx \mathbf{6666.67 \text{ mm} = 6.67 \text{ m}}$$

**(iii) World Coordinates (2 marks):** Since $v_L = v_R = 240 = c_y$, the point lies on the principal horizontal plane, so $Y = 0$.

For $X$: From $u_L = f \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$:

$$X = \frac{(u_L - c_x) \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{(412 - 320) \times 6666.67}{800} = \frac{92 \times 6666.67}{800} \approx \mathbf{766.67 \text{ mm}}$$

Therefore: $\boxed{(X, Y, Z) = (766.67 \text{ mm}, 0, 6666.67 \text{ mm})}$

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Choice 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the fundamental matrix? Derive the epipolar constraint $p_L^T F p_R = 0$ from first principles, starting from the essential matrix. Explain the eight-point algorithm for estimating $F$ from point correspondences.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the steps of Zhang's planar camera calibration method. Explain how the intrinsic matrix $K$ is recovered from multiple homographies using the orthonormality constraints on the columns of the rotation matrix.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The fundamental matrix $F$ is a $3 \times 3$ rank-2 matrix that encodes the epipolar geometry between two cameras in *raw pixel coordinates* (i.e., without requiring camera calibration). It is related to the essential matrix by:

$$F = K_R^{-T} E K_L^{-1}$$

**Derivation:** Starting from the essential matrix constraint in normalized coordinates, $x_L^T E x_R = 0$, and substituting $x = K^{-1} p$:

$$(K_L^{-1} p_L)^T E (K_R^{-1} p_R) = 0$$

$$p_L^T K_L^{-T} E K_R^{-1} p_R = 0$$

Defining $F = K_L^{-T} E K_R^{-1}$ yields the **fundamental matrix constraint**:

$$\boxed{p_L^T F p_R = 0}$$

This is a scalar equation. The fundamental matrix has 7 degrees of freedom (9 entries − 1 scale − 1 rank-2 determinant).

**Eight-Point Algorithm (4 marks in summary):** Given $N \geq 8$ point correspondences $(p_L^i, p_R^i)$:
1. For each correspondence, form a 9-element vector from the outer product: $\mathbf{f}_i = (u_L u_R, u_L v_R, u_L, v_L u_R, v_L v_R, v_L, u_R, v_R, 1)^T$.
2. Stack the vectors into a matrix $A$ of size $N \times 9$.
3. Solve $A \mathbf{f} = 0$ via SVD: $A = U \Sigma V^T$. The solution is the column of $V$ corresponding to the smallest singular value.
4. Enforce the rank-2 constraint: replace the smallest singular value of the resulting $F$ with zero (SVD of $F$ followed by reconstruction).
5. Optionally refine via nonlinear minimization of the **Sampson error** (geometric distance to the epipolar lines).

**Model Solution for (b):**

Zhang's method calibrates a camera using a planar checkerboard target viewed from multiple (≥3) orientations.

**Step 1 — Homography Computation (1 mark):** For a planar target at $Z = 0$, the projection is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = H \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

For each view, $H$ is estimated from the 2D-2D correspondences between checkerboard corners.

**Step 2 — Orthonormality Constraints (3 marks):** Since $r_1$ and $r_2$ are orthonormal columns of a rotation matrix:
- $r_1^T r_2 = 0 \implies h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0$
- $r_1^T r_1 = r_2^T r_2 \implies h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_1 = h_2^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2$

Let $B = K^{-T} K^{-1}$ (a symmetric $3 \times 3$ matrix with 6 unknowns). These constraints become linear in the 6 entries of $B$:

$$h_i^T B h_j = v_{ij}^T b = 0$$

where $b = (B_{11}, B_{12}, B_{22}, B_{13}, B_{23}, B_{33})^T$ and $v_{ij}$ is constructed from the elements of $h_i$ and $h_j$.

**Step 3 — Solve for B (1 mark):** Stacking the constraints from $n$ views gives $V b = 0$. Solve via SVD: $b$ is the right singular vector of $V$ with smallest singular value.

**Step 4 — Extract K (1 mark):** Cholesky-factor $B = L L^T$ where $L = K^{-1}$ (lower triangular), then invert to get $K$:

$$K = L^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Extract Extrinsics (1 mark):** From each $H$:

$$\lambda = 1 / \vert\vert K^{-1} h_1 \vert\vert, \quad r_1 = \lambda K^{-1} h_1, \quad r_2 = \lambda K^{-1} h_2, \quad r_3 = r_1 \times r_2, \quad t = \lambda K^{-1} h_3$$

**Step 6 — Refinement (extra credit):** Nonlinear optimization (Levenberg-Marquardt) minimizing the total reprojection error to refine $K$, all $(R_i, t_i)$, and to estimate lens distortion coefficients $(k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Units Mismatch in Depth Calculation:** Students frequently mix units — using $f$ in pixels and $B$ in meters, or vice versa. The depth equation $Z = fB/d$ requires $f$ and $B$ in the **same units**. (Lose up to 2 marks.)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Principal Point Offset:** When applying $Z = fB/d$ to a *non-canonical* setup, students often forget that $d$ must be computed as $d = (u_L - c_x) - (u_R - c_x) = u_L - u_R$ *only* when $c_x$ is the same for both cameras. If the principal points differ, the disparity must be computed using the offset-adjusted coordinates.
>
> 3. **Confusing the Skew Parameter $s$ with Shear:** The skew parameter $s$ in $K$ is often misidentified as a shear transformation. It is actually a manufacturing artifact of the sensor and is zero for nearly all modern cameras.
>
> 4. **Confusing Essential and Fundamental Matrices:** The essential matrix operates in *normalized* (calibrated) coordinates; the fundamental matrix operates in *raw pixel* coordinates. Mixing them up is the #1 conceptual error in Part B answers. (Lose up to 3 marks.)
>
> 5. **Eight-Point Algorithm Incomplete Explanation:** Students often list the steps but forget to enforce the **rank-2 constraint** on $F$ after the initial SVD solve. This is a 1-mark deduction in most KTU valuations.
>
> 6. **SfM vs. SLAM Confusion:** SfM is *offline* and operates on unordered image collections; SLAM is *online* and processes a video stream in real time. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the Sign of Depth in Triangulation:** The reconstructed 3D point $Z$ must be **positive** (in front of the camera). If $Z < 0$, the cheirality check has failed and the chosen $(R, t)$ from the four essential matrix decompositions is wrong.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**
>
> - **Stereo Vision** is depth from two views using the principle of **triangulation**.
> - The **disparity-depth relation** $Z = fB / d$ is the single most important equation in this module.
> - **Depth error grows as $Z^2$** — this is why stereo has limited range and why long-range systems use other sensors.
> - **Camera calibration matrix** $K$ has 5 DOF (or 6 with skew): $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, s$.
> - **Full camera projection:** $p \sim K[R \mid t] P_w$ — this combines intrinsic ($K$) and extrinsic ($R, t$).
> - **Epipolar geometry** reduces correspondence search from 2D to 1D along the **epipolar line**.
> - **Essential matrix** $E = [t]_\times R$ uses **calibrated (normalized)** coordinates and has **5 DOF**.
> - **Fundamental matrix** $F = K^{-T} E K^{-1}$ uses **raw pixel** coordinates and has **7 DOF**.
> - Both $E$ and $F$ have **rank 2** and encode the same geometric relationship in different coordinate systems.
> - **Eight-point algorithm** estimates $F$ from ≥8 correspondences via SVD, then enforces rank-2.
> - **Four-fold ambiguity** in $(R, t)$ recovery from $E$ is resolved by the **cheirality check** (3D points in front of both cameras).
> - **Zhang's calibration** uses a planar checkerboard; recovers $K$ from orthonormality constraints on the rotation matrix columns.
> - **Bundle adjustment** is the global nonlinear refinement of all camera poses and 3D points to minimize **reprojection error**.
> - **SfM pipeline** = Feature detection → Matching → Geometric verification → Pose initialization → Incremental registration → Triangulation → Bundle adjustment → MVS densification.
> - **SfM** is *offline*; **SLAM** is *online* and real-time.
> - **Triangulation** recovers 3D from 2D-2D correspondences; **PnP** recovers pose from 3D-2D correspondences.
> - **Stereo rectification** aligns epipolar lines with image rows, enabling fast row-by-row correspondence search.
> - **Stereo Block Matching** computes disparity for every pixel by minimizing a matching cost (SAD, SSD, NCC) over a local window.
> - **Sub-pixel refinement** (parabolic fit, Birchfield-Tomasi) is essential for high-accuracy depth maps.
> - **Levenberg-Marquardt** is the standard nonlinear optimizer for bundle adjustment and camera calibration refinement.
> - **Reprojection error** $\vert\vert p - \hat{p} \vert\vert^2$ is the fundamental loss function in all geometric vision problems.
> - **RANSAC** is used to robustly estimate $F$ and $H$ in the presence of outlier correspondences.
> - **Lowe's ratio test** (ratio of nearest to second-nearest neighbor descriptor distance < 0.7) filters ambiguous feature matches.
